data.title: Trump warned credit card crackdown will trigger financial crisis for millions
data.description:
Donald Trump’s crackdown on credit cards risks pushing millions of poor Americans into financial crisis, some of Wall Street’s most powerful groups have warned.

Mr Trump is pressing ahead with plans to temporarily cap credit card interest rates for 12 months at 10pc from January 20.

However, the proposal is facing a backlash over fears it will force banks to cancel cards and pull back lending to consumers with the lowest credit scores.

On Monday, the American Bankers Association (ABA), which represents major lenders like JP Morgan, hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman and the Bank Policy Institute (BPI) all rounded on Mr Trump over the plan.

Mr Ackman, who has publicly supported Mr Trump, added: “My concern about capping rates at 10pc is that doing so will inevitably cause millions of Americans to have their cards cancelled as credit card companies lose the ability to adequately price subprime credit risk.

“Consumers denied credit cards will be forced to turn to loan sharks whose rates and terms will be vastly worse for borrowers.”

The BPI said the cap would “be devastating for millions of American families and small business owners … the very consumers this proposal intends to help”. The ABA said the move would push consumers towards “less regulated, more costly alternatives”.

Mr Trump’s announcement sent shares in US credit card providers sliding on Monday, with Capital One leading the fallers plunging by as much as 6.24pc.

In the UK, as much as £8bn was wiped off the market value of British banks. Shares in Barclays, which has a US credit card business, fell by 3.3pc in London. Shares in NatWest were also down by 1.2pc, even though the bank does not operate a US credit card business.

The plan has stoked widespread fears because credit cards are so vital for the US economy, with the number of accounts hitting a record 642 million in the third quarter of 2025.

This means the US has nearly twice as many credit card accounts as it does people. Total credit card debt is at a record $1.23tn (£891bn) – up by 39pc since the pandemic began.

In theory, capping at 10pc should dramatically reduce borrowers’ costs because it would more than halve the average rate paid, which was 22.5pc in November.

At no point since at least 1994, when Federal Reserve data began, has the rate been as low as 10pc. According to Vanderbilt University, a 10pc cap would save US consumers $73bn net a year.

However, analysts warn it is precisely the people who Trump is trying to help that will be hit the hardest by his rate cap because it will force banks to do less lending.

Justin Begley, economist at Moody’s Analytics said: “If the government does indeed enforce this policy on the banks, then it’s just going to become completely unprofitable to lend to people that are of higher credit risk.

“I would disagree with people who are saying that the banks can absorb this. The banks have to be able to properly price credit risk.”

Banks may simply cancel subprime borrowers’ credit cards outright, Mr Begley added. “That’s something I have heard banks talking about doing. They’re going to want to protect themselves.”

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said: “It is likely that lower rates will push credit providers to reassess their lending criteria and the policy ends up creating more harm than good.”

Subprime borrowers have a credit card default rate of 1.2pc, double the overall default rate, according to Moody’s. Currently, subprime borrowers make up 21pc of accounts in default. During the financial crisis, the rate was closer to half.

The measure will hit at a time when low and middle income US households are becoming increasingly overwhelmed with their debts.

The share of Americans who are falling seriously behind on their credit card payments has already soared to a near 15-year high.

Debt advice firms have been warning that America’s cost of living crisis is increasingly hitting middle class households, as families turn to credit just to cover the costs of basic necessities.

data.title: Shutting down Britain because of bad weather is pathetic
data.description:
When we touched down at Heathrow on Saturday, and the pilot warned of “a big freeze” in the UK – with temperatures “sinking as low as -3C” – my daughter and I fell about laughing. It had been -16C in New York, and the wind blasts were such that you felt the cold not just in your bones, but your entrails – such that you could never imagine feeling warm again.

Did anybody (but us) mention the weather? Nope. In their duvet coats, balaclavas and snow boots, hardy New Yorkers somehow got on with their lives. They took buses and trains that somehow worked, despite inches of snow. They made it to work on time and did the jobs they were paid to do. With what little emotion my frozen limbic system was still able to produce, I felt awed by their grit – by the collective functionality of the Empire City.

Can you imagine any visitor saying that about us? Not if the Trades Unions Congress (TUC) has anything to do with it. We’re not yet a week into 2026, and already, the TUC has embarked on a concerted campaign to “Stop Britain Working” – and by “working”, they also mean functioning.

There’s the light dusting of snow, for starters, which has instantly prompted the usual hilariously overblown rhetoric. No one should be “pressured into making dangerous journeys”, the TUC warned on Sunday, and workers “must not” be penalised if they cannot get to work. 

When “snow causes problems on the UK’s transport network,” it had previously said, “it makes sense for employers to encourage and enable staff to work from home if they can, rather than struggle with a lengthy, risky and difficult commute to and from work”.

Yes, we can’t have any struggling. And it goes without saying that nothing in professional life should ever be either lengthy or difficult. Grit is something to be scattered on icy roads (if the scatterers can be bothered), not a quality to aspire to. Although it should perhaps be pointed out that the Met Office advice was slightly more sanguine. People, it said yesterday, should “plan to leave the house at least five minutes earlier than normal”.

Once the Arctic freeze and the “travel chaos” are over, and once we’re done talking about just how Arctic and chaotic it really was, the TUC would like us to focus on a more enduring component of their “Stop Britain Working” campaign – and that is, of course, “stress”. That little gem of a word – the opportunist’s gift that keeps on giving.

You may not be aware of this, but we are, in fact, in the middle of a “national work-related stress crisis”, and the unions are demanding urgent intervention. In a recent TUC survey of 2,700 safety representatives, four out of five reported stress as a primary concern, with many respondents claiming that “excessive workloads” are driving that stress to unprecedented levels.

TUC general secretary, Paul Nowak, warned on Sunday that: “These findings expose a growing national crisis. Stress is now entrenched as the biggest health and safety issue facing working people, and the situation is getting worse.” 

He added: “No worker should find themselves lying awake at night from stress, but too many employers are ignoring the law, failing to assess stress risks, and piling impossible workloads on to staff. Workers are burning out, and they are paying with their health.”

“Getting worse” – really? Are we genuinely saying that we are more stressed and overburdened in our working lives than any previous generation? Because that’s quite a bold claim. One that – I think most of us can agree – isn’t supported by external evidence.

I understand that stress was and is a real thing (that has since been hijacked by the work-shy). I understand that it is bad for us and that we should try and avoid it, wherever possible. But the idea that we somehow have it worse than ever before? 

The notion that what Britain needs right now is to work less hard, to cut itself some slack, to take a few more duvet days and indulge in some wellness journaling on the couch? I don’t actually have a comeback for that.

I will, however, say this. I don’t believe that we are an inherently feeble country. I know we’re not. Which is what makes this assumption of feebleness 10 times worse, because assumed feebleness creates true feebleness, and when you look at the rash of headlines over the first week of the year – “One in Ten British Workers Wants To Resign This Month”, “No Point in Working Harder: The Rise of Part-time Britain” – it’s not terribly heartening.

Do we really want feebleness to become our unique selling point? Because that really would be a national crisis.

data.title: Debanking soars to record high
data.description:
Have you been debanked? Email money@telegraph.co.uk.

Almost half a million customers were debanked last year, the highest figure in a decade, as account closures continued to spike.

An estimated 453,230 accounts were shut down, according to figures released to The Telegraph under Freedom of Information rules. This was a more than tenfold increase on the 45,091 accounts closed in 2016-17, and an 11pc jump on the 408,000 accounts closed in the 2023-24 tax year.

In all of the cases, lenders cited “financial crime reasons” for the decision to close accounts.

Nigel Farage, who was himself controversially debanked by private bank Coutts in 2023, described the figures as “appalling” and blamed European legislation, which he claimed “makes it cheaper for banks to close accounts over unusual transactions”.

It comes ahead of the introduction of new rules which will force lenders to give customers longer before they close accounts – at least 90 days’ notice – as well as offer clearer explanations of why they shut the accounts.

But the requirements will only apply to accounts opened after April 28 this year and will be subject to exemptions to allow banks to comply with financial crime rules.

Financial institutions are allowed to close accounts for commercial reasons, and if they suspect criminal activity. There is no legal right to a bank account in the UK, unlike in countries such as France and Belgium.

Banks have faced criticism over their failure to explain to customers exactly why accounts have been closed. This is often blamed on anti-money laundering and other financial crime rules which lenders must follow.

Anti-money laundering rules were substantially tightened in 2017, requiring banks to monitor customer activity and check identities. The rules mean that banks which suspect customers are engaging in criminal activity cannot tell them why their accounts are being closed.

The National Crime Agency estimates that £10bn is laundered through the UK’s banking systems every year.

In December, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) fined Nationwide £44m for ineffective warning systems, and for knowing that some customers were breaching its own terms.

In one case, Nationwide allowed a customer to receive 24 payments totalling £27.3m over 13 months into a personal current account. HMRC was unable to recover £800,000 of the payments.

Since 2021, the city regulator has imposed 13 fines on banks for failing to observe money laundering rules, totalling more than £300m.

Another pressure point for lenders is rules introduced in October 2024 which require them to reimburse victims of authorised push payment (APP) fraud.

In the first half of last year, lenders repaid in 88pc of cases, totalling more than £84m, with the vast majority of refunds paid out within days. Experts previously told The Telegraph that the reimbursement rules could be driving higher numbers of account closures.

James Graham, a senior researcher at think tank the Prosperity Institute, said: “The continued rise in account closures, as shown by this new data, shows that the debanking epidemic remains a present and real danger for increasing numbers of British citizens.

“Preventing crime sounds noble, but nobody believes another 453,000 people are financial criminals, on top of the 408,000 in 2024, and 317,000 in 2023.”

Maxwell Marlow, of think tank the Adam Smith Institute, said: “The scourge of debanking continues to blight the British public and significantly impacts the Square Mile.

“Our finance system thrives through freedom – it is why we were the centre of global capitalism for so long, and we became rich from it. If we choose to reject these principles by ignoring this issue, our liberties and prosperity will be punished collectively.”

A UK Finance spokesman said: “Banks must comply with strict legal and regulatory requirements in terms of verifying customers and preventing financial crime. As a result, a small proportion of accounts are closed, but only after extensive review and investigation.”

A Treasury spokesman said: “Debanking is wrong, which is why we’ve put protections in place for millions of people and small businesses.

“Under the new rules, customers will receive at least 90 days’ notice and an explanation for any account closure, strengthening protections which prohibit banks from discriminating against someone based on their political opinions.”

An FCA spokesman said: “Fighting financial crime is one of our priorities. Fraud makes up over 40 pc of crime in the UK, robbing people of their hard-earned money.

“It’s important banks and building societies play their part, including closing accounts they have suspicions about. Only a tiny fraction of accounts are closed – and we expect firms to act proportionately and treat customers fairly.”

data.title: Drivers could be fined years after speeding
data.description:
Drivers face being fined years after breaking the speed limit under Labour’s road safety plans.

Ministers are planning to remove the six-month time limit on legal action for speeding offences, which has resulted in thousands of cases being cancelled.

Under the plans, the six-month time limit will only start when police have been informed who was driving a vehicle that broke the speed limit or went through a red light, which can sometimes take months.

The Government argues that the requirement for alleged offenders to receive a court summons within six months is “open to abuse and delaying tactics” and “can result in offenders avoiding justice”.

If a driver contests a fine, some alleged offenders can wait 18 months for a magistrates’ hearing because of court backlogs.

But Nick Freeman, one of Britain’s top motoring lawyers, told The Telegraph that the proposed new system would only result in further delays, and that some motorists accused of speeding could wait years for the result of legal action.

He said the Government’s plan was a “lame excuse to cover for their own inefficiencies”, arguing that “the only people abusing this system are the prosecuting authorities”.

“There has to be a time limitation,” he said. “The purpose of this legislation is so that people can genuinely remember who was driving the vehicle, but the problem is the authorities are not efficient. They need to get their act together.”

Mr Freeman, who runs his own legal firm and is known as “Mr Loophole” for his success in defending celebrities convicted of driving offences, said further delays would result in slower justice for innocent people.

“We don’t want people to be prosecuted for a red light offence three years later,” he said.

The current system requires police to issue a suspect with a Section 172 notice, which requires them to confirm who was driving the vehicle at the time of the alleged offence.

Within 28 days, the owner of the vehicle must then confirm that they were driving at the time, or give police details of the person who was behind the wheel. The second person is then given a further 28 days to respond.

After receiving the information, police are then required to ask a magistrates’ court to issue a summons. All stages of the process must be completed within six months for the prosecution to continue, and courts often issue a summons on the final day of that period.

The Government does not routinely publish data on the number of speeding prosecutions abandoned by prosecutors, although analysis by the RAC Foundation in 2022 found that 17 per cent were scrapped for a variety of reasons, including that prosecutors exceeded the six-month limit.

Speeding offences are usually punished with a fine as a percentage of a driver’s weekly income, up to a maximum of £1,000. Offenders can also be ordered to participate in speed awareness courses.

Labour’s road safety plan also includes a plan to give people who drive without wearing a seatbelt three points on their driving licence, in addition to the current penalty of a fine. The rule will also apply to passengers under the age of 14, who are legally the responsibility of the driver.

Ministers have already been criticised for the plan to lower drink-driving limits, which hospitality bosses said will reduce footfall in rural pubs.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save Britain’s pubs after a series of measures by Labour left thousands of businesses questioning whether they would be able to stay afloat.

In response, Lillian Greenwood, the road safety minister, said the new rules were not designed to prevent the public “having a great night out”.

“What we’re just saying is don’t take your car,” she told Times Radio. “So that might mean that, you know, some places you’ll be able to take a bus or a taxi. In other places, you’re going out with a group of mates, one of you agrees to be the designated driver.”

A Department for Transport spokeswoman said: “This consultation will seek all views before any decisions are made.”

data.title: How Heathrow got back on schedule
data.description:
As Heathrow rode a post-Covid travel boom to reclaim its title as Europe’s busiest airport in 2022, it also secured a less welcome crown – the region’s worst offender for delays.

Just three years on, and while Heathrow remains Europe’s leading airport, it is also among the most punctual after a dramatic drop in late arrivals and departures.

Central to this unlikely transformation has been hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in industry-leading IT systems and the latest terminal and airfield equipment.

This has coincided with a rare outbreak of peace between Heathrow and its biggest airlines, British Airways (BA) and Virgin Atlantic.

After a long history of conflict with Heathrow management, a newfound level of cooperation has enabled a rethink of procedures that had long hindered punctuality.

New figures from aviation analysts Cirium show that almost 80pc of flights departed from Heathrow on time last year, an 8pc improvement compared with 2024.

That makes the west London hub the fourth-most punctual airport in Europe, behind only much smaller terminals in Oslo, Copenhagen and Stockholm, and far ahead of its biggest rivals.

Amsterdam and Frankfurt, with which Heathrow competes for lucrative long-haul traffic, saw just 65pc of flights leave on time, while Paris-Charles de Gaulle languished at 62pc.

Heathrow also recorded its busiest December on record last month, helping yearly passenger numbers hit an all-time high of 84 million.

Meanwhile, Virgin Atlantic and BA cut arrival delays by almost 10pc last year, the best performance among airlines worldwide, according to Cirium.

Suzanne Roddie, the chief operating officer of Sir Richard Branson’s Virgin Atlantic, says the transformation represents a coup for the UK’s aviation sector and the wider economy.

She says: “Last year was the time when we saw the really meaningful improvement that technology investment and data investment can drive.

“We are often one of Heathrow’s biggest challengers, but my view is that we should be proud of it coming fourth in Europe. It’s the only hub airport in the UK, and we need it to perform.

“When it comes to operations, we work really collaboratively, and that partnership really doubled down last year to support punctuality and reliability.”

Heathrow’s performance is a far cry from the dark days of 2022, when cancellations and delays led to chaotic scenes as the airport failed to cope with resurgent demand, pushing bosses into the unprecedented step of capping flights.

At the height of summer that year, nearly 40pc of flights landed more than half an hour late, and 19pc were more than an hour late – not factoring in thousands that were scrapped altogether.

Heathrow euphemistically suggested that its performance had “dipped”, while John Holland-Kaye, then chief executive, was pilloried for claiming it had “delivered a great passenger experience to the vast majority of travellers”.

All told, just 59pc of services took off on time throughout 2022, leaving Heathrow far adrift from its European peers and widely touted as an airport basket case.

Dale Reeson, Heathrow’s director of operations, says the airport’s resurgence has stemmed from the airport and airlines acknowledging the extent of the problem and being prepared to invest to solve it.

He says: “There are examples of airports that are really punctual around the world, and we really wanted to be counted among those.

“So we established a team focused solely on improving all aspects of punctuality, pulled a plan together, got sign-off from the airlines and rolled it out at the beginning of 2025.

“We kept speaking once a month with the airlines about how the plan was going and keeping that performance drumbeat up.”

Heathrow has tapped artificial intelligence to improve flight planning, from a year out to within 20 minutes of departure, he says.

Reeson also says there has been a push to identify the most damning factors behind delays.

‘An absolute game changer’

The airport also became the first in the world to deploy a software system that calculates the minimum safe separation between planes arriving, aimed at reducing the number of flights entering holding patterns.

The technology, known as Pairwise and developed by air traffic controller Nats, reduced arrival delays by almost 80pc and allowed Heathrow to squeeze in one extra flight per hour.

This is crucial owing to the constraints of Heathrow’s two runways, which will remain at capacity until the airport’s planned £49bn third runway is up and running in the next decade.

Reeson says: “It’s world-leading now, what happens here. We have maximised the utilisation of our runways to a level I don’t think anyone has seen before.

“It’s an absolute game changer and the foundation of the punctuality improvement because it means arrivals are much closer to their scheduled time, which makes it easier for everyone.”

The contribution of airlines

The next stage was to persuade airlines to inform the airport in advance of anticipated departure delays so they could be accounted for in the system.

He says: “Airlines were providing last-minute updates when all that did was push them to the back of the queue. We asked them to update their information early and get it right.

“If you won’t be ready for a half-past flight until 40 minutes past, then tell us. If you’re ready early, we’ll push you forward. It’s about being a little bit more accurate and pessimistic with your times so that you don’t get that additional delay.”

Mr Reeson says that while Heathrow has moved faster to embrace cutting-edge technology, he expects rival airports to follow suit.

Heathrow’s recent success has coincided with continental hubs facing their own problems.

Amsterdam has been battling a government clampdown on flights. Paris-Charles de Gaulle is disproportionately affected by strikes, and Frankfurt experiences frequent summer storms.

Tom Woodworth, the head of operations planning at British Airways, says the carrier, which operates half of the flights at Heathrow, also deserves credit for the airport’s turnaround after spending hundreds of millions of pounds on boosting resilience.

He says: “If you were to look at BA’s performance a couple of years ago, you’d actually see that we were, on average, worse than other airlines there, whereas now we’re better.

“The fact that we are now helping to drag up the average rather than dragging it down tells us we are doing the right thing. Charles de Gaulle, Frankfurt and those other places have remained pretty static, whereas we were a long way behind and are now ahead.

“We’re not there yet, but we are really pleased with having achieved such a big improvement in a relatively short space of time.”

Woodworth says BA has prioritised technology to boost performance, introducing new check-in systems and utilising AI to allocate the thousands of tasks needed to keep the airline running each day.

He says: “We do still have challenges with closed airspace due to strikes or thunderstorms or whatever else.

“These things are always going to happen, and you have to be resilient when dealing with them and making sure that you can continue to run your programme.

“But things that you may previously have seen as unpredictable and far beyond even the cleverest person, you know what, you can predict them with AI, and if you can predict them, you can begin to control them.”

data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.

data.title: The German plot to woo Rolls-Royce
data.description:
Sitting in front of an enormous prototype jet engine, a German minister beams at the camera as he signs a cooperation deal with two top Rolls-Royce executives.

“With this, both sides commit to actively shape the transformation of aviation,” a social media post boasted gleefully afterwards.

Dietmar Woidke, the president of the German state of Brandenburg, had every reason to feel cheerful at November’s signing ceremony.

In the past decade alone, his state has attracted hundreds of millions of pounds in investment from Rolls and positioned itself as a “centre of excellence” for small-business jet engines.

Now it is jockeying for an even bigger prize – and a chance to swipe some of Britain’s industrial crown jewels.

In recent months, German officials have mounted a charm offensive to secure more work related to Rolls’ next-generation jet engine, the UltraFan.

Rolls-Royce Dahlewitz hub, outside Berlin, has already played a key role in developing what is arguably the new turbofan’s key innovation: its gearbox.

But the Germans are understood to be pushing hard for Dahlewitz to play an even larger role in the future, based on an offer of potentially generous subsidies.

The timing of these lobbying efforts is not coincidental either, coming as Berlin scrambles to defend itself from an avalanche of Chinese cars that threaten to crush Germany’s once-unstoppable motor industry.

Worryingly for Sir Keir Starmer, Rolls appears to be listening.

While the company is understood to favour keeping development in Britain, insiders insist it is duty-bound to consider all options – particularly as it is accountable to shareholders, not the Labour Government in London.

One well-placed industry source said: “The Germans are already involved in UltraFan. The only question now is what they do beyond that.”

Such a loss of investment by the company, widely regarded as a national champion, would deal a serious blow to Starmer’s industrial strategy and risk the loss of valuable know-how to foreign factories.

“It’s a very sensible thing for Rolls-Royce to consider – and potentially very bad for the UK,” warns Rian Whitton, an industry analyst at Bismarck Analysis.

The German advances have come at a critical moment, just as Rolls is considering a return to the fast-growing market for “narrow-body” planes, which have a single aisle and are used by carriers such as Ryanair for short-haul flights.

Rolls-Royce stopped producing narrowbody engines more than a decade ago. Embarrassingly, this meant the company completely missed a boom in demand for best-selling aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

But the company’s UltraFan demonstrator offers a route back in, thanks to the engine’s scalability.

Under the leadership of Tufan Erginbilgic, the company is preparing to spend £3bn on developing a narrowbody UltraFan demonstrator.

Rolls is understood to be seeking hundreds of millions of pounds in subsidies for the programme and has raised the issue at the highest levels of Government.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, have visited the company’s Derby headquarters multiple times and used its factory as a backdrop for major announcements.

UltraFan could be a game-changer for several reasons, including improving overall efficiency by around 10pc compared to today’s engines. Engineers believe the design can be scaled up or down, meaning it could be used to power both narrow and so-called “wide-body” aircraft, which have two aisles between the seats.

Insiders acknowledge that the technology improvements also have clear potential applications in defence, such as increasing the capacity or flight time of air tankers, or enabling more compact fighter jet engines.

At a time of global instability and questions about the reliability of the US, such sovereign capabilities are seen as increasingly important.

Still, Germany has already played a crucial role in the development of the first UltraFan demonstrator.

Dahlewitz engineers co-created the power gearbox in a joint venture with domestic company Aerospace Transmission Technologies.

At the same time, Dahlewitz is where Rolls develops and manufactures the Pearl range of business jet engines and is developing new, even smaller engines for “loyal wingman” drones.

However, state and federal government officials are urging Rolls to consider assigning the German site a role in other parts of UltraFan as well.

That would be controversial in Britain, with sites such as Derby currently responsible for designing and developing sections, including the fans, core and combustion chamber.

Within these specialisms lie some of the most valuable and closely guarded secrets in the British aerospace industry.

Jobs in this field are also high-value and distributed geographically across the UK, with any loss of production to foreign factories likely to be politically controversial.

“These jobs are a relatively small component of overall employment, but they are disproportionately high productivity,” Bismarck’s Whitton adds.

“They are important to exports and research and development spending, so they should arguably be prioritised over measures that just increase employment more generally.”

‘Aviation is becoming an important offset’

But Germany is equally desperate to pull them in, as its traditional strongholds are increasingly threatened by a flood of cheap goods from China, says Sander Tordoir, chief economist at the Centre for European Reform.

“German chemicals and carmaking, which is still Germany’s most important industry, are really in deep trouble,” he says.

“So aviation is becoming an important offset – it’s a sector that’s actually growing.”

According to the German Aerospace Industries Association, the number of aerospace jobs rose to 120,000 in 2024, up from around 105,000 a decade earlier.

The country’s aviation industry has long been anchored by Airbus, which conducts final assembly of some aircraft there and also manufactures fuselage sections, cabins, and wing parts domestically.

Germany is also home to Rolls-Royce’s power systems business, which manufactures diesel and gas generators, as well as engines for ships and trains, among other things.

But the country currently lacks a champion capable of making complete commercial jet engines – a weakness that has left it exposed in a row with France over who will get to make what for a next-generation warplane.

This might also partially explain why German officials are so keen to build up this particular muscle, along with the threat from China and the high value of aviation jobs, says Tordoir.

“Airbus is a German-British-French-Spanish collaboration and has done very well. But of course, that’s not how politicians will think of it in the short run,” he adds.

Certainly, some alarm bells should be ringing in Whitehall, according to Bismarck’s Whitton.

But he questions whether ministers and their officials are prepared to act, despite Rolls having recently secured substantial work funded by UK taxpayers.

Rolls was recently chosen by the Government to design the country’s first mini nuclear power plants and has separately won orders for specialist military reactors that will be needed for the Royal Navy’s Dreadnought and Aukus-class submarines.

But in some ways, the fact that Rolls is the sole supplier of some of these technologies gives the Government less leverage in the dispute, not more.

There is also little cash available for handouts, given the constrained state of public finances, whereas in Germany, the government is committing significant spending to defence and infrastructure.

“I think in France, this just wouldn’t happen,” says Whitton. “In part because [national aviation champion] Dassault is family-run and has significant state ownership.

“But unfortunately, Whitehall does not have a tremendously strong hand to play here.

“We talk about Rolls-Royce as a British crown jewel, but that’s got to be heavily caveated. Today it is a fairly globalised, export-led, publicly traded company that does not have a primary loyalty to the British state.”

Still, he argues ministers should fight hard to keep these jobs here.

“They need to have a tough conversation with them,” he says.

The Government has so far attempted to dismiss such concerns. A spokesman said the UK has one of the most competitive aerospace sectors in the world and that it values “the role Rolls-Royce continues to play, supporting thousands of high-quality, high-wage jobs”.

Yet with Germany on Britain’s tail, ministers may soon wish they had paid more attention.

A Rolls-Royce spokesman said the company had a “long-standing relationship with the State of Brandenburg” and looked forward to “building on our successful collaboration in the future”.

data.title: Send the Army into Tunbridge Wells to fix water crisis, Starmer urged
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer has been urged to send the Army into Tunbridge Wells in response to a crisis that has left 30,000 homes and businesses without water.

Residents across Kent and Sussex have called for the Government to take emergency action after taps in the area ran dry for the second time in as many months.

South East Water, the utility company responsible for supply, has been heavily criticised for its handling of the successive crises, with bottled water running out at emergency supply points and a lack of communication with locals.

Dry Wells Action, a Tunbridge Wells community group set up in response to the shortages, is now calling for the Army to be sent in to take over the crisis response.

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident running the campaign, said: “This is a logistics problem. South East Water has demonstrated that they can’t move water from one place to another – they have an ever-changing catalogue of excuses.

“The army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in crisis situations. We need unprecedented help.”

Water stations set up to provide bottles to affected locals have so far been chaotic. One in East Grinstead was open on Saturday for just an hour and a half before running out of bottled water.

‘Nobody believes them now’

East Grinstead Town Council said the response from South East Water was poorly coordinated.

A spokesman said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town.

“To the best of the Council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations on Saturday, contributing to significant traffic congestion and disruption in the town centre.”

Judy Guest, a South East Water customer and member of the Dry Wells Action campaign, said the company’s response to the crisis had been “carnage”.

She said: “We get these updates via text. Nobody believes them now. We need to have a sort of an independent review as to what’s going on because I think we just don’t have faith.”

Since last Tuesday, South East Water is understood to have provided water to some residents each morning only to cut it off at lunchtime.

Mr Hawker added that he was outraged by South East Water boss Dave Hinton’s appearance in front of Parliament last week. Asked to rate his response to pre-Christmas issues that left 24,000 customers without drinkable water for a fortnight, Mr Hinton gave himself an eight out of 10.

Mr Hawker said: “Any organisation or leader who gives themselves eight out of 10 for their handling of a situation where they fail to do the only job they have – supplying water to people – on the day they cut off supplies again is seriously deluded.”

He added that he would “struggle to give Hinton a one out of 10”.

South East water has said the latest issues were caused by Storm Goretti, as well as low reservoir levels. 

A Government spokesman said they will be calling on Ofwat to investigate South East Water.

A source close to Emma Reynolds, the environment secretary, said that Ms Reynolds has been shocked by Mr Hinton’s lack of accountability since shortages started at the end of November.

data.title: Sexual AI images to be criminalised in crackdown on Musk’s Grok
data.description:
Ministers are to criminalise the creation of sexual AI “deepfakes” in a crackdown on Elon Musk’s service Grok.

Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary, said the Government would this week bring into force a new offence criminalising the creation of sexualised non-consensual AI images.

Ofcom, the technology and media regulator, will also be given new powers to pursue companies that allow the images on their sites, Ms Kendall said.

The new offence threatens to step up a row with Mr Musk over his social network X – the platform formerly known as Twitter.

Ofcom said on Monday it had launched a formal investigation into X over its AI service Grok creating and sharing images of women and children wearing bikinis or in sexual poses.

The Government passed laws last year to make it illegal to create sexually explicit deepfakes, but the offence had not yet come into force.

Enforcing it from this week will put X under pressure to rein in Grok, which has generated thousands of digitally manipulated images of women, including the Princess of Wales, in states of undress.

Ms Kendall said she would also make creating the images a priority offence under the Online Safety Act, meaning sites such as X would have to prove they are taking steps to prevent the images.

She said: “No woman or child should live in fear of having their image sexually manipulated by technology.”

The Technology Secretary suggested X was breaking the law.

She said: “Let me be crystal clear, under the Online Safety Act, sharing intimate images without someone’s consent or threatening to share them, including images of people in their underwear, is a criminal offence for individuals and for platforms.”

Despite this, she said that the Government would not stop using X to communicate with the public. 

Ms Kendall said that the Government would keep its use of the social network “under review” but that “the facts need to be heard wherever possible”.

It came after Ofcom took a step towards banning X, launching an investigation into the company’s artificial intelligence undressing women and children.

The regulator said it would investigate whether the site was complying with the Online Safety Act over “deeply concerning reports” the AI bot was making sexualised images without people’s consent.

Ministers have threatened X with a ban in recent days, leading Mr Musk, who owns X, to describe the UK as “fascist”.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “Reports of Grok being used to create and share illegal non-consensual intimate images and child sexual abuse material on X have been deeply concerning.

“Platforms must protect people in the UK from content that’s illegal in the UK, and we won’t hesitate to investigate where we suspect companies are failing in their duties, especially where there’s a risk of harm to children.

“We’ll progress this investigation as a matter of the highest priority, while ensuring we follow due process. As the UK’s independent online safety enforcement agency, it’s important we make sure our investigations are legally robust and fairly decided.”

Ofcom first made contact with X a week ago, giving it a deadline of Friday to provide evidence about how it was dealing with online safety laws. It said it had launched the investigation after an “expedited assessment” of this evidence.

It said it had the power to block X from the UK if the company did not comply with its investigation.

Alternatively, it can order social networks to take steps to address problems, or impose fines of up to 10pc of a company’s worldwide turnover. In X’s case this could amount to hundreds of millions of pounds.

The regulator said it would investigate X on six issues, including whether it had stopped people in Britain seeing illegal content and whether it had assessed the risk to children using the service.

Ofcom said it would provide updates on the investigation as soon as possible.

Writing in The Telegraph this weekend, Ms Kendall said she backed Ofcom using its full legal powers, including a ban, to tackle “appalling” images created by Grok.

Writing on X at the weekend, Mr Musk said the Government wanted “any excuse for censorship”. After the investigation was announced, he wrote: “Censorship is the hallmark of an actually fascist state.”

In response to Mr Musk’s claims, a spokesman for the Prime Minister said: “We’re fully committed to the right to free speech and upholding that right. Whilst every country has the right to set its own rules, we support the laws and institutions which we’re working to keep the Internet free from the most harmful content.”

An investigation could lead to further concerns about retaliation from the US. Donald Trump has already suspended a tech deal with Britain amid concerns about overzealous online safety regulation.

Sarah Rogers, Donald Trump’s free speech tsar, has compared the UK to Russia in contemplating a ban on X.

data.title: The middle-class battle to hire (and keep) domestic help
data.description:
Emma Horton has been “ghosted” by two cleaners in the past year.

“One seemed really keen, came a couple of times but then never returned and stopped responding to messages,” says Horton, 48, who lives in Cambridgeshire and owns a PR company. “Another did a similar thing, while the third kept cancelling at the last minute for various reasons, each one more far-fetched than the last, so I let her go. I’ve given up trying to find one.”

Call it a first-world problem but finding someone to clean your house seems more difficult than ever these days.

Several times a week, my local WhatsApp group, which numbers almost 650 people across Essex and Suffolk, pings with a variation on the same message: “Help! Can anyone recommend a good cleaner?”

It’s not only cleaners. Sourcing gardeners is a problem, too – just ask the King. Staff shortages, low pay and the monarch’s exacting demands have reportedly led to an exodus from Highgrove House, his Gloucestershire residence. Since 2022, 11 out of 12 garden staff have left, including two head gardeners.

And then there’s the battle for builders and tradesmen. Often, people report trying to get quotes from as many as seven or eight builders – in many cases, they never hear back and, if they do, the quotes are often eye-wateringly expensive.

According to the latest state of trade survey by the Federation of Master Builders (FMB), 61pc of businesses report being affected by skilled labour shortages. This, in combination with rising materials prices and increased wage and National Insurance costs, means that 61pc of companies surveyed by the FMB have also increased the prices they charge for their work.

“Since Covid and Brexit, households have found themselves competing for cleaners, gardeners and tradesmen in a way they simply didn’t have to before,” says Robin Edwards, of London buying agency Curetons.

“It was a double whammy, as Covid accelerated people leaving the workforce and changed work preferences, while Brexit reduced the pool of migrant labour that had previously supplied many domestic service roles.”

The recruitment crisis means that, in many parts of the country, securing household staff can feel like a competitive sport, with homeowners increasingly willing to fight dirty.

“In the Cotswolds, we have seen people offering cleaners cash on the spot to jump ship from a neighbour, while village politics have also erupted into full-blown feuds over the loyalty of a particularly talented gardener,” says Harry Gladwin, of buying agency The Buying Solution.

“Increasingly, success in the countryside is measured not just by the splendour of your house but by whether you have managed to hold on to the cleaner who knows how to coax red wine out of a Cotswold stone floor.”

A homeowner from Essex says the cleaning company she used would often cancel just before the cleaners were due. “This was usually because the manager had received a call from the council for an emergency clean or even from the police for specialist forensic cleaning, which was presumably better paid,” she says. “I clean after the cleaners have been, but feel I have to keep them on as it’s better than nothing.”

According to official data from the Office for National Statistics, the number of people employed as “cleaners and domestics” has actually risen over the past year or so. After dropping sharply from 491,800 in 2021 to 460,300 in 2022, it recovered last year to 492,200.

Crucially, however, the survey only picks up people who work for employers registered for PAYE and VAT. As such, many will probably be cleaning commercial premises such as schools and hospitals, and the numbers will not reflect the thousands of cleaners who work mainly for private households being paid in cash.

And this is where the worst shortages are to be found. The British Cleaning Council (BCC) says recruiting cleaners is particularly difficult in London, where 60pc of the industry workforce was born overseas. Other regions have historically relied much less heavily on migrant labour – in north-east England, for instance, only 4pc of those working in cleaning were born overseas, the BCC says.

“We met a new client recently who has just lost their trusted Bulgarian cleaner of many years as she decided to move back home – and we’re hearing this more and more,” says Simmi Sangha, of property company Eccord, which manages 150 homes across London.

“Those who are staying are increasing their hourly rates and most clients are paying without hesitation because they don’t want to risk losing them.”

While the most recent ONS figures, dating from April 2024, showed that the median pay for those employed as full-time cleaners was £12.12 an hour, anecdotal evidence suggests households are paying much more than this – in London and the South East £25 an hour is common. With some cleaners in the capital now reportedly running waiting lists for clients, homeowners are going to increasingly desperate measures to attract and keep them.

“Lavish birthday and Christmas presents and bonuses are also common,” Edwards says. “I know of one client who lent their housekeeper the free use of their villa in Portugal for a week and another, an aesthetic doctor, who gives his cleaner free Botox.”

There’s also a recruitment crisis in horticulture, with the number of gardeners and landscape gardeners falling by 29,000 between 2019 and 2021, according to the ONS’s Labour Force Survey.

The sector relied heavily on seasonal workers from the EU before Brexit, while people in their 50s and 60s are retiring and are not being replaced by new apprentices. Fewer young people are willing to embark on a career that’s often hard work but poorly paid, despite the extensive knowledge needed for skilled roles.

Qualified self-employed gardeners will charge an average rate of between £30 and £41 an hour, according to research by the Gardeners Guild, although this assumes you book them for a full day and are a regular customer. Irregular customers may expect to pay more.

In some cases, they face paying significantly more. A client of Eccord in Chelsea, south-west London, was recently quoted £600 plus VAT per month for a gardener to come in once a fortnight to do a basic tidy up of a small terraced rear garden and a few window boxes.

Meanwhile, Jennie Hancock, of the West Sussex buying agency Property Acquisitions, has a client who bought a house and paid a “significant premium” to persuade the gardener to stay on. “This wasn’t only because he was excellent and knew the garden inside out, but he’d also worked on the Capability Brown gardens at nearby Petworth Park, which brought a certain cachet,” she says.

And they’re the lucky ones. It took Katie Neale, from the buying agency Haringtons UK, five attempts before she could even get a gardener to reply to her requests to come and work at her own rural home. “When he finally came, after an hour of wandering around with his hands on his hips, he declared the garden ‘a life’s work’ and promised a quote that never materialised,” she says. “I now see him every week at my neighbour’s house, where he ducks behind the hedges whenever I drive past.”

And don’t get Emma Horton started on builders. She and her husband would like to knock a wall through from their kitchen to the dining room, add bifold windows in the kitchen and put a utility room into part of the garage. Yet, just as for cleaners, she’s been ghosted by two builders and told by a third that he can’t do the job for another year.

“We have another company coming round and I am hopeful that we might get somewhere, but the process is so frustrating and time-consuming,” she says. “I wonder whether it’s because it’s not a particularly huge job, but I wish they would just respond, even if it’s to turn us down.”

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, says it has long been tricky to hire a builder as construction has never kept up with demand. “Good ones are booked up 12 to 18 months ahead and that has always been the case – if anyone said they could start next week, I’d run a mile,” he says. “Cost pressures have become more pronounced since Covid particularly, and builders are a lot better paid than people realise.”

And spare a thought for the wealthy, who are also battling for staff to look after their houses and gardens. The 2025 Estate Staff Salary Survey by Knight Frank estate agency, released in June, found that 87pc of estates and country houses said it was becoming harder to attract good staff, while almost 60pc said retaining workers was also becoming more difficult.

This comes despite years of steady wage increases. The average salary of an estate director has risen from £83,224 in 2020 to £103,221; a housekeeper’s pay has gone from £24,292 to £30,426; and a head gardener’s income has risen from £28,806 to £36,428, according to Knight Frank.

There are several factors to blame, including rural locations that are a barrier to recruitment, says Alastair Paul of Knight Frank’s rural consultancy.

In the countryside, the Covid-era race for space that suddenly ratcheted up the demand for rural living was also a factor, although that has now calmed down, points out Philip Eddell, the director of Eddell & Co property consultancy.

It’s not uncommon for roles for housekeepers, gardeners and maintenance teams to sit empty for months, says Kathryn Donkin, of Strutt & Parker. “I know an estate that has been advertising for a maintenance worker for nearly a year,” she adds.

data.title: Wall Street tycoon’s attack on City funds is ‘bad for Britain’
data.description:
Boaz Weinstein’s assault on City funds risks damaging the UK’s attractiveness to investors, the chairman of Edinburgh Worldwide Investment Trust (EWI) has said.

Jonathan Simpson-Dent, the chairman of EWI, has hit out at the US hedge fund tycoon as it rallies against an activist campaign from his firm, Saba Capital.

He said Mr Weinstein’s offensive against UK investment trusts risks impacting confidence in the investment sector, which could make it harder for trusts to attract employees.

EWI is among a group of trusts targeted by Mr Weinstein in the past year, as the American has sought to unseat a series of boards over poor performance.

Other trusts under pressure from Saba include Herald Investment and the Baillie Gifford US Growth Trust.

‘An unpleasant campaign’

Tensions escalated with EWI last week when Saba, which owns 30pc of shares in the fund, threatened to sue if it failed to provide more information regarding its decision to sell down its stake in Elon Musk’s SpaceX.

Speaking to The Telegraph on Monday, Mr Simpson-Dent said: “This has been an unpleasant campaign. He said some nasty things and a lot of people don’t want that.

“If the candidate pool significantly diminishes because people see this as a really difficult and high-risk place to play personally, then I think that’s got some medium to long-term harm.”

Saba is proposing to remove all six of EWI’s independent non-executive directors and appoint three of its own US-based independent nominees. The resolutions will be put to a vote at the trust’s upcoming general meeting on January 20.

A victory for Saba would also send the wrong message about the UK, Mr Simpson-Dent added.

He said: “The UK equity markets are already under some element of stress and seeing outflows over the course of the last couple of years.

“It doesn’t send a great message that we’re open for business and the UK is a great place to come and invest.”

Rather than simply pressuring EWI to improve performance, Mr Simpson-Dent said he believes that Saba’s goal could be to take over the entire trust.

Mr Simpson-Dent said: “They’re really trying to build their franchise in the UK and they want to grab a platform vehicle to allow them to do that.”

The chairman of EWI added: “In this world of capital markets, if someone wants to take over something, they make an offer that would then be considered by boards and shareholders, usually at a premium to the current trading value.

“There’s inherent value in the portfolio that you’d expect them to be analysing to figure out what premium was merited, but they don’t want to, they just see an opportunity to get it on the cheap.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Starmer’s rail nationalisation risks returning Britain to 1970s, warns Labour MP
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s blueprint for nationalising the railway risks dragging the network back to the 1970s, one of his own MPs has warned.

Scott Arthur, a member of the Commons transport select committee, said that making Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, the ultimate arbiter in disputes concerning Great British Railways (GBR), could politicise the most minor decisions.

He said that would prompt scrutiny on a par with British Rail (BR) in the 1970s, when even the state of its notoriously unpalatable sandwiches became the subject of political debate.

Mr Arthur, the Labour MP for Edinburgh South West, said at a meeting of the select committee: “For GBR to succeed it has to have some independence from the Government, it has to be at arm’s length.

“With the Secretary of State having so much discretion and so much input over it, is there not a danger that it’s just going to become politicised?

“We’re going to end up having urgent statements on how fresh the sandwiches are on the railway like we used to do back in the old days.”

Sandwiches sold on British Rail services in the 1970s, often served soggy after being bound in cling film, were a political football and staple of comedy shows for decades.

The sandwiches became a source of frustration for British Rail, generating more headlines than innovations such as the High Speed Train.

Prue Leith was hired to oversee an overhaul in 1977, but the sandwiches still featured prominently in a privatisation debate a decade later, with then Tory MP Teresa Gorman calling for one to be preserved as a symbol of the state-run railway.

Mr Arthur told MPs on Wednesday that handing powers currently held by the Office of Rail and Road to the Transport Secretary also risked undermining public confidence in nationalisation.

Rail-freight firms and the handful of private passenger operators that will carry on providing so-called open access services have claimed that GBR will inevitably favour its own services when allocating paths for trains, and that Ms Alexander is likely to back its decisions.

Mr Arthur said: “The Secretary of State is accumulating all these powers. As a member of the public, there’s no transparency there if I’m concerned about the decision-making process.

“There’s no way for me to understand how decisions are reached if it’s the Secretary of State that’s guiding the process and she’s one of the partners within the process.”

He said that while some people might be “excited to hear the ORR will be monitoring the situation,” the public would prefer it to have “more teeth.”

Mr Arthur said he was also concerned that Ms Alexander would be able slash targets for boosting freight operations at her own discretion, and questioned why the railways bill currently passing through Parliament includes no targets for passenger numbers.

He said: “Is there not going to be a temptation that to reduce the cost to the taxpayer they simply reduce services and end up with fewer passengers on the railway?”

Lord Hendy, the rail minister, said the idea that the Government would become more hands on following nationalisation was “fatuous” and that once train and track operations were united under GBR, he intended to be less closely involved with day-to-day matters.

He told the committee: “I don’t want to answer these letters about why the 8.24am from Richmond didn’t run this morning. I want them to be answered by someone who is actually responsible.

“The people running GBR will have nowhere to go because GBR will be responsible for everything. I want them to wake up and say, ‘This is all my fault and we have got to fix it.’”

He said the railway had even proved “incapable” of implementing a new timetable on the line from London Kings Cross to Edinburgh despite £4bn of investment, forcing him to take on a duty that would fall to a minister nowhere else “outside North Korea and the old USSR”.

data.title: BT could face investigation after digital switch leaves customers without phone line
data.description:
Are you a BT customer experiencing problems because of the digital switchover? Tell us about your experience at money@telegraph.co.uk*

BT could face an investigation after its digital rollout left some vulnerable customers without access to a phone line.

Regulator Ofcom said it could investigate and potentially fine the company if bungled landline cut-offs put customers at risk.

The telecoms giant is currently rolling out “Digital Voice”, a new system that will scrap traditional copper landlines and make all phone lines digital by 2027.

However, the provider has been accused of a botched rollout after people were left without any connectivity over Christmas. Pensioners and vulnerable residents in rural communities risked being left without access to emergency services, and businesses were left without an income.

Ofcom requires providers to have policies in place to ensure vulnerable customers are treated fairly, and says the ability to contact emergency services is a “critically important function” of networks.

Tony Farrar, 78, from Nottinghamshire, said his relatives were unable to wish him a merry Christmas after his landline was disconnected in December.

BT reassured Mr Farrar there would be no change to his phone number or contract. Days later, he received a new contract in the post, and was soon notified his number would change.

When his Digital Voice was installed on Dec 16, Mr Farrar’s connection was cut off altogether.

The retiree was told his connection would return on Jan 2 but he was not reconnected until Jan 6.

“I have spent hours on the telephone since October over this. The fact that they were just ignoring it was beyond belief,” Mr Farrar said.

“This time of year I have family, friends calling on the landline. They couldn’t get through, we were not getting those calls.

“I’m fairly compos mentis, but I am 78 and I know many people my age who wouldn’t have a cat in hell’s chance of dealing with this situation. It’s not fair on people, particularly when it’s not something they’re asking to be done.”

MPs have raised concerns that the Digital Voice rollout could leave rural customers without access to emergency services in the event of a blackout.

Mr Farrar added: “There’s going to be a lot of older people who struggle with this. They can’t make 999 calls and they’ve been messed about.”

A BT spokesman apologised and said the company was in touch with Mr Farrar to restore his connection as quickly as possible.

Joe Goodhart, 90, from East Yorkshire, couldn’t reach a family member in a difficult circumstance over Christmas because his landline with third-party reseller Love Business Group for business broadband – which is provided by BT – was cut off. BT told The Telegraph that it no longer manages Mr Goodhart’s phone line and therefore could not reconnect their landline.

Mr Goodhart said: “The telephone was just cut off one day – we picked up the phone to use it and it was dead. That was six weeks ago and the phone is still cut off now. It has interfered with our family.”

The crisis puts BT at risk of investigation by Ofcom, which fined Virgin Media £23.8m in December when vulnerable customers were put at risk by a bungled digital landline switchover.

The Virgin Media blackout meant customers with so-called “telecare” devices – personal alarms designed to be activated in a health emergency or after a fall – were unable to contact emergency services.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “We have clear rules and guidance that phone companies must follow when switching customers to digital landlines. Providers must minimise disruption, communicate effectively with customers, and support people who might be vulnerable. If we see evidence of widespread issues, we’ve shown we’ll step in.”

“Earlier in December, we fined Virgin Media £23.8m for putting vulnerable customers at risk of harm during its programme to migrate customers to digital landlines.”

A report published by the Digital Communities parliamentary group in March warned migrations could have “deeply personal repercussions” on vulnerable older and disabled people.

Anna Sabine, the group’s Lib Dem member, told The Telegraph: “It is disgraceful and dangerous that vulnerable people were left without access over Christmas.

“We warned that the safety net wasn’t there, and it is unacceptable that retirees and families in remote areas have paid the price for this botched rollout during the most isolating time of the year.”

Matt Barker, 58 from Oxfordshire, opted to move from landline to fibre connection in August when BT indicated it would reduce the couple’s monthly bill. They said they lost months of business at their stained glass restoration company when BT cut off their landline, meaning customers who called their business number couldn’t get through.

Mr Barker said: “The engineer [who installed fibre connection] assured us the landline, which we use as our work phone, would be working by the end of the day.

“But it never worked. We rang BT, we sent several emails – it took about 12 weeks. When they finally sent an engineer out, he said he only worked with the old copper system and couldn’t help us.

“I got a message out of the blue in mid December saying Digital Voice was now connected, and the phone worked – but it really should have happened the day they changed us over.

“We know we missed business. We had someone on our mobile saying they’d been trying to get hold of us for weeks and weeks on our landline. And I wasted hours of time.

“I’m in my late 50s, I’m not really au fait with tech, but my daughters are and they couldn’t understand it either.”

A BT spokesman said, “We’ve investigated Mr Barker’s complaint, and we can confirm that Mr Barker requested an upgrade with us and this request was completed. Mr Barker hasn’t contacted us to report a loss of service, which we’d be more than happy to support with. We are trying to reach Mr Barker to resolve his complaint.”

The crisis could also spell legal trouble for BT, a retail law expert has warned.

Dean Dunham, a consumer lawyer, said: “Whilst BT is entitled to carry out the switch from analogue landlines to Digital Voice, a change which has been signalled for years and is happening across the industry, this does not give it a free pass to leave customers without a working phone service for weeks or months.

“If people are paying for a landline and it isn’t working, particularly over an extended period, BT risks being in breach of contract.

“There are also heightened obligations when it comes to pensioners, vulnerable customers and anyone who relies on a landline for safety or emergency contact. Ofcom rules are clear that these customers must be identified, supported and protected during the switchover.

“Where vulnerable people have been left cut off over Christmas, that raises serious questions about whether those obligations have been properly met.”

A BT spokesman said it has been raising awareness of the switchover for the past two years.

A statement said: “We’ve reviewed the customer cases shared with us. Our investigation indicates delays in Mr Farrah’s and Mr Barker’s switchovers were linked to number transfer and setup issues, some involving other suppliers. We’re working with both customers to resolve these issues. Mr Goodhart’s enquiry relates to a business phone line service provided by a third party.

“We continue to encourage our customers experiencing issues to contact us directly so we can review their setup and provide the best solution for their needs.

“For customers with additional needs, we offer free battery back-up units, hybrid phones and in-home assistance. We’re also investing in improving mobile network resilience in rural areas.

“Anyone with questions or concerns about the switchover should contact their landline provider, who can make sure they have the right solutions in place.”

Two in five UK adults have a landline at home, with a quarter of households using it regularly, a GoCompare survey conducted via YouGov found in February last year.

Catherine Hiley, a broadband expert at GoCompare said: “Not everyone in the UK has home broadband. And digital phones may stop working during a power cut unless there’s a backup solution.

“Landline providers should contact customers ahead of the switchover to explain the process and any steps needed to keep services running smoothly.”

data.title: Tube stations shut down as staff jet off on summer holidays
data.description:
London Underground stations closed for a combined total of more than 45 days over the summer holiday season because of staff shortages, it has been revealed.

Stops on the Tube network were forced to shut down 387 times during normal hours in the months of June, July and August in 2025 – three times as many times as in the spring.

Underground stations in the capital were closed for a total of 65,786 minutes during the period – the equivalent of 45 days, 16 hours and 26 minutes, according to Transport for London (TfL) data.

Station closure figures covering the last five years, obtained following a freedom of information request by The Telegraph, show large seasonal discrepancies for disruption caused by staff shortages.

The findings have prompted critics to call on TfL to “get a grip” and better monitor worker rotas during popular holiday months.

It comes as Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, prepares to increase Tube fares by an inflation-busting 5.8 per cent.

‘Not good enough’

Susan Hall, leader of the London Assembly Conservatives, said it was “ludicrous” that station closures occurred more frequently during the summer.

“It’s about time TfL started considering the customers,” she said. “It’s very inconvenient when a station closes; people have got to get to work.

“It’s not good enough, especially when fares are going up.”

Between January 1 2020 and October 2025, there were nearly twice as many closures in summer (1,562) as there were in total during the spring months of March, April and May (802).

Chancery Lane station was the worst affected after being closed to force 64 times, while Lancaster Gate closed 54 times and Covent Garden on 39 occasions.

Regulations require some stations to have a minimum number of staff, meaning TfL is legally obliged to close a station if worker numbers are insufficient.

During shortages, workers are moved to well-connected stations with higher usage and step free access in a “prioritisation” process, The Telegraph understands.

As a result, stations that do not meet this criterion are closed “more frequently”.

Network ‘not properly staffed’

Elliot Keck, campaigns director at the TaxPayers’ Alliance, said: “These figures raise serious questions about how London’s transport network is being run.

“While staffing levels have increased, closures keep piling up. That strongly suggests the network still isn’t properly staffed to cope with sickness and holidays.

“Londoners should not be expected to tolerate routine closures because basic cover hasn’t been put in place. TfL needs to get a grip.”

Nick Dent, TfL’s director of customer operations, said: “We are sorry if customers have been affected by short-notice closures at some of our Tube stations.

“We are aware of the inconvenience that station closures can cause, which is why we only close stations as a last resort and always work to minimise the length of time a station is closed for.”

He added: “We do all we can to minimise station closures, and we have an established and flexible rostering system to ensure stations are sufficiently staffed throughout the year.

“We are also introducing a new staffing model that will help reduce station closures by enabling us to deploy staff more flexibly.”

data.title: Musk’s X risks advertiser boycott over ‘misogynistic’ AI chatbot
data.description:
Elon Musk’s X is facing a potential boycott from advertisers which have attacked the social media site’s “misogynistic” AI chatbot.

Sir Keir Starmer has urged media regulator Ofcom to consider banning X in Britain after it emerged that the site’s Grok chatbot is being used to undress women in photographs and generate child sexual abuse images.

But Mr Musk’s company could face a more imminent threat to its top line as advertisers sounded the alarm over online safety, raising the prospect of a brand exodus.

Simon Michaelides, director general of the Incorporated Society of British Advertisers (ISBA), which represents brands including Mars, Unilever and British Airways, said: “We and our members are just as shocked as everyone else.

“There is absolutely no place ever for the kind of misogynistic and CSAM [child sexual abuse material] content that Grok is clearly churning out at the moment.”

Mr Michaelides said that while it was not for ISBA to tell brands how to spend their money, the trade group advised members on how to avoid reputational damage from running adverts alongside harmful material, which he described as a “nightmare scenario”.

The advertising chief added: “I think there is now an inherent caution about X as a platform because it is the one that seems to constantly pop up and cause caution.”

X, formerly known as Twitter, suffered a collapse in advertising revenues following Mr Musk’s $44bn (£33bn) takeover of the social media platform in 2022, as brands baulked at the billionaire’s decision to tear up content moderation policies.

Accounts published this week show the company’s UK revenues dropped by almost 60pc in 2024 to just under £29m. It admitted that the decline was driven by a “reduction in spend from large brand advertisers due to concerns about brand safety, reputation and/or content moderation”.

Threats from Musk

Nevertheless, Mr Musk has adopted an aggressive response to the advertising boycott, telling companies who withdrew from the site to “go f--- yourself”. He has also filed a lawsuit against brands including Mars and Unilever over claims that they illegally conspired to withhold advertising revenues.

Unilever was dropped from the lawsuit after reaching an undisclosed settlement with X. Mars and other brands have asked a judge to dismiss the claims, accusing Mr Musk of using it to win back business “lost in the free market”.

X’s advertising revenues appear to have remained resilient so far. Retail advertisers in the US increased their ad spend by more than 30pc in the final week of 2025 as they capitalised on post-Christmas sales, according to data from Sensor Tower. By contrast, advertisers in the financial services, gaming and media and entertainment sectors reduced their spending.

However, the figures predate a furore over thousands of so-called deepfake images generated by Grok.

X users have instructed the chatbot to create pictures of women and children undressed, including in sexualised poses and in bikinis. This has included images of the Princess of Wales, cabinet ministers, MPs and celebrities.

Ofcom has said it is considering an investigation into X, with the Prime Minister calling for “all options to be on the table”, including fines of billions of pounds or blocking access to the site in Britain.

Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary, urged Ofcom to issue an update within days, adding that should the watchdog decide to ban X it would have the Government’s “full support”.

Mr Michaelides said advertisers were “very supportive” of the intervention by ministers and regulators. He added: “This is a violation of the Online Safety Act and therefore it’s absolutely right that Ofcom and Government are getting involved.”

Grok has now limited image generation to paying subscribers. However, Downing Street dismissed the move as “insulting”, saying it had merely turned a chatbot that generates illegal images into a premium service.

X has been contacted for comment.
