data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Diesel lorries to be banned in eco drive
data.description:
Labour has unveiled plans to introduce a petrol and diesel lorry ban as part of their net-zero drive.

Ministers have announced an end to the sale of new fossil fuel-powered trucks in a move that opens up a new battle with the Tories and Reform.

They have ruled out allowing the continued use of low-carbon or synthetic fuels, meaning that from 2040 all new heavy goods vehicles (HGVs) will have to be electric.

Critics warned that forcing haulage companies to adopt expensive battery or hydrogen-powered vehicles would push up the cost of business.

The extra expense would then be passed onto consumers in the form of higher prices for goods like food and clothes, as well as parcel deliveries.

Richard Holden, the Tory shadow transport secretary, said: “Labour are sleepwalking into a cost shock for the entire economy.

“Forcing haulage firms to scrap perfectly good lorries while they can still run for years and replace them with other vehicles before either the technology or infrastructure is ready will simply drive up costs, as well as being environmentally disastrous.

“Those costs will be passed straight through supply chains into higher prices in shops, higher construction costs, and more pressure on inflation. Every supermarket shelf, building site, and home delivery will feel higher prices.

“Rather than letting innovation and the market cut emissions sensibly, Labour are reaching straight for quotas, caps and bans. Central planning with a delivery charge attached is delivering another blow to the British economy.”

The move risks exposing Labour to further attacks from the Tories and Reform, who have both pledged to roll back costly net zero policies.

Both parties have said they would scrap the 2030 petrol car ban and cut subsidies, which would cost taxpayers billions a year.

The 2040 date for phasing out petrol trucks was first proposed by the Tories in November 2021 at the height of Boris Johnson’s net zero ambitions.

In a consultation document published last week, the Department for Transport set out three options for enforcing the new petrol lorry ban.

The first would see manufacturers set annual quotas for the number of electric HGVs they must sell, similar to how the petrol car ban will work.

Another option laid out by ministers is to follow the EU’s approach and set haulage companies ever decreasing carbon emissions caps.

Finally, delivery firms could be required to ensure that an ever increasing proportion of their fleet was electric, eventually reaching 100 per cent.

Chris Ashley, from the Road Haulage Association (RHA), said: “We’re clear that the decarbonisation transition hinges on two key issues being addressed – infrastructure in place to power zero emission HGVs, and vehicle affordability.

“The pathway to decarbonise lorries and coaches in the UK must be realistic and viable – we’ll look to see how the consultation achieves that.”

In a survey run by the RHA last year, seven in 10 haulage firms said they had no plans to add any zero-emission vehicles to their fleets. The main barriers cited were the extra costs involved in buying electric-powered lorries and concerns over their lack of range.

Figures from February last year show that there were just 500 electric HGVs registered in the UK out of a total fleet of 535,000.

Extra subsidies needed

Separately, the Green Finance Institute has estimated £100bn worth of additional finance and subsidies would be needed to support electric lorry take-up.

More than 80 per cent of goods are transported around Britain by road, meaning an increase in cost for hauliers could have a significant impact on prices.

The logistics sector also employs almost 2.7 million workers, with its more than 200,000 businesses adding £185bn to the UK economy every year.

For smaller trucks up to 26 tonnes, the sale of new petrol and diesel-powered models will be banned from as early as 2035 under the plans.

Keir Mather, the minister for transport decarbonisation, said the Government was aware it would be “challenging” to decarbonise the sector. But he insisted there was a need to act because lorries “have a high environmental impact” and emit 16 per cent of all emissions from domestic transport.

“That is why we are firmly committed to accelerating the transition to clean technologies and to setting a path to phase out the sale of…all new non-zero emission heavy goods vehicles by 2040,” he wrote.

He claimed technological advances would lead to “ever better, cheaper and more capable vehicles” that would ultimately bring down costs for haulage firms.

data.title: Electrical equipment shortage threatens net zero, Hitachi warns Miliband
data.description:
A shortage of electrical equipment threatens to derail Ed Miliband’s flagship net zero targets, a leading manufacturer has warned.

Hitachi, the world’s leading producer of high-voltage transformers, said a critical lack of kit could leave UK power operators unable to expand the grid fast enough to achieve clean electricity by 2030.

Laura Fleming, Hitachi’s UK managing director, said this shortfall was caused by unprecedented demand for electricity combined with a shortage of raw materials and skilled labour.

This has led to soaring prices and delayed deliveries for electrical equipment crucial to net zero.

Ms Fleming said: “In the UK and Europe, we’ve had 30-40 years of growing these [electrical] systems only on an as-needed basis and we’ve actually not seen electricity consumption go up dramatically.

“But we are now in a very different place where we’ve got very ageing systems with ageing equipment that needs replacing.

“At the same time, we’re trying to expand that grid because we’re moving from an energy system that is predominantly oil and gas-based to an energy system that is much more based on renewables, nuclear and a handful of other technologies.

“This is creating pressure on the system in a number of ways.”

The scale of the planned changes to Britain’s energy system is huge, inspired by the Energy Secretary’s move to embrace green electricity and slash the country’s reliance on fossil fuels.

Currently, the UK’s power stations, wind farms and other electricity producers have a total generating capacity of about 100 gigawatts.

This comes from a diverse mix of generators, ranging from massive gas-fired or nuclear power stations that produce steady, reliable power to intermittent wind and solar farms dependent on the weather.

Transformers are the unseen technology that unifies this system, converting the power they produce into the standardised voltages and frequencies needed by the UK’s power networks.

They work by “stepping up” power to the high voltages needed to efficiently transmit it long distances, typically around 400,000 volts.

Then, at the other end of the line, they reduce it back down to the 240 volts we need to run our homes.

But the 500 or so supergrid transformers that form the backbone of Britain’s high-voltage power transmission system are no longer enough to meet the nation’s needs.

Mostly installed between five and six decades ago, their age is showing – as highlighted by the catastrophic substation fire that shut down Heathrow last year, subsequently traced to a faulty 60-year-old transformer.

There are also far too few of them to support Mr Miliband’s ambitions of decarbonising the entire grid by 2030, along with a significant increase in power generation by 2050.

He has pledged to displace the oil and gas that now supply over 75pc of our total energy with green electricity from wind, solar and nuclear plants.

Such promises are easy to make but require mammoth upgrades to Britain’s grid.

Transformer capacity is measured in megavolt-amperes (MVA), with the UK currently boasting a capacity of about 170,000MVA.

By 2035, that will have to reach up to 280,000MVA – a two thirds increase – with even more needed by 2050.

That’s an unprecedented increase – and Britain’s problem is that its plans to expand and update its transmission network have coincided with everyone else’s across Europe.

The UK has three transmission operators: National Grid covering England and Wales, Scottish Power covering southern Scotland and SSE covering northern Scotland.

Recently, all three have found themselves pitched primarily against Tennet, the Dutch-German grid operator, for power transformers, subsea cables and other vital equipment.

For example, Britain wants to connect 21GW of offshore windfarms to its grid while Tennet has its own plans for 40GW of new wind.

The resulting power struggle has seen transformer order times surge from six months to four years, with prices rising 75pc in real terms since 2019.

Ms Fleming said it’s not just demand for raw materials that is driving up costs but the risk involved and the debt needed to finance new factories.

“Prices have gone up across the piece but it’s not just the costs for materials that have gone up,” she said. “It’s also the cost of finance. From a developer’s point of view, that probably adds more cost than the overall commodity costs.”

A 2025 report from the International Energy Agency set out the scale of the problem.

“Transformer lead times have almost doubled over the past two to three years,” it said. “Manufacturers have reported a record backlog of orders, increasing by one third in just one year.”

Artificial intelligence’s role

Hitachi is just one of several major players but its transformer backlog has now risen to $43bn (£32bn) – a trend being accelerated by the rise of AI.

Ms Fleming points to an internal Hitachi report – “Gridlocked? AI’s energy appetite” – which quantifies the impact of AI on the demand for transformers and associated equipment such as cables.

It calculates that Europe, including the UK, will need up to 200 giant new transformers by 2030 just for its new data centres, while America will install up to 460 and China another 130.

Add in the rest of the world and the total comes to 1,400 new transformers by 2030, just for AI alone.

Ms Fleming said this means that Britain faces a triple challenge.

It has to upgrade its old grid, expand it to meet the demands of decarbonisation and then expand it again for the needs of AI.

That’s a challenge but it could also be an opportunity – a chance for Britain to lead the world in manufacturing the parts needed for a future powered by green electricity. Will Britain grab that opportunity?

Serving the UK from Europe

Last month, regulator Ofgem announced its final approval for £90bn of investments proposed by National Grid, SSE and Scottish Power in pylons, substations and transformers, with the costs added to household and business energy bills.

So far, however, it’s clear that most of the kit needed to upgrade and expand the grid will be made abroad.

Ms Fleming said that most of the Hitachi transformers deployed in the UK will come from its factory near Łódź in Poland, assembled from components made in countries such as Sweden, Spain and Finland.

Meanwhile, the reluctance of investors like Hitachi to invest in the UK is largely related to its turbulent politics, especially around net zero.

For example, until the 2024 election, the target for decarbonising the grid was 2035 – a date chosen because of the long lead times needed for such massive infrastructure projects.

When Mr Miliband moved that date to 2030 after Labour came to power in July 2024, it prompted a radical political shift.

Notably, it destroyed the decades-long political consensus on climate in Westminster, a reversal that has seen the Conservatives row back from green policies that they introduced.

It also further opened the door to Reform, which has pledged to abolish net zero targets altogether.

In an interview last spring, Ms Fleming said that, faced with such turbulence, Hitachi could not take the risk of investing in UK factories.

“What we require beyond 2030 to invest in the UK is longer-term visibility,” she said. “So the visibility until 2030 is great but to be able to start considering longer-term investment, that visibility needs to go well beyond the political cycle.”

Such concerns surrounding the UK are already costing swathes of jobs and hundreds of millions of pounds in investment.

At Hitachi’s transformer factory in Sweden, for example, the company has doubled the workforce from 4,000 to 8,000 – about 1,000 new jobs a year, many to build transformers for the UK.

As Bruno Melles, Hitachi’s head of transformer manufacturing, puts it: “Right now, we believe that we can serve Great Britain from the investment that we made in Europe.

“The major factories supplying Britain are in Poland, Sweden, Germany, Spain and Italy. I have to say, all our factories are very, very active to ensure that we can supply Great Britain in a proper way – from Europe.”

A Government spokesman said: “Upgrading our electricity networks after years of underinvestment is essential to keep the lights on and ensure energy security for our country.

“We work closely with transmission operators who have robust long-term supply plans to secure essential components like transformers and with industry to make the most of Great British Energy’s £1bn plan to strengthen UK clean energy supply chains.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why banning X would be harder than Labour imagines
data.description:
How do you solve a problem like X? For Britain, the answer might appear seemingly simple: ban the site altogether.

Sir Keir Starmer has launched a full-scale assault as Westminster seeks to rein in Grok, the chatbot owned by Elon Musk’s social network – formerly known as Twitter.

On Friday, ministers said they would “fully support” any move by UK regulators to ban access to X and the Grok chatbot. 

The offensive, in response to Grok being used to undress pictures of women and children, suggest the website could be wiped off the face of the map if Musk fails to act over the deepfake scandal.

But a ban would be an extraordinary step – and harder to enact than first appears.

While the number of Brits using X has fallen under Musk’s ownership, seven million adults still use the site every day, according to figures from Ofcom.

And it would generate a backlash from users and almost certainly lead to retaliation from the White House, where Musk has close ties thanks to his friendship with Donald Trump.

It would also see Britain join a ignominious club, whose other members include China, Russia, Iran and North Korea.

Grok, X and Musk are in trouble because the chatbot has been creating tens of thousands of realistic images of women, and in some cases children, in minimal clothing or in sexual poses.

These deepfake images are typically altered versions of real people, with women including the Princess of Wales re-drawn at the request of users.

In response, Sir Keir and female celebrities such as Maya Jama have urged X to disable the feature – but seemingly to no avail.

While the company has removed the function for the public, it has bundled the feature into its subscription package – to users who fork out $30 a month.

The last resort

If Britain follows up with the nuclear option and outlaws X, it would fall to industry watchdog Ofcom to wield the knife.

The watchdog was given new online safety powers last year allowing it to block websites in the UK.

In recent months, the regulator has already raised the prospect of blocking other websites that fall foul of the laws.

In response to the deepfake scandal, Ofcom has threatened X with an online safety investigation, saying it had made urgent contact with the company and that it was aware of “serious concerns” that the bot was generating sexualised images of children.

“We will undertake a swift assessment to determine whether there are potential compliance issues that warrant investigation,” the regulator said.

Under the Online Safety Act, Ofcom has powers to force websites and apps to shield children from adult and harmful material, and all users from illegal content.

Sites available in Britain must check users’ ages to stop children seeing pornography and prevent illegal content such as child abuse images.

Powers can range from censure and fines to “business disruption measures”, such as requiring payment providers and advertisers to cut off services.

Ultimately, these could also apply to internet service providers such as BT, ordering them not to connect users to the site.

This is seen as a last resort, but it is not out of the question.

In a separate case, Ofcom said this week that it was preparing to seek a court order that could block an unnamed suicide forum in the UK, after it refused to provide the regulator with a risk assessment that websites are required to submit under online safety laws.

‘Insulting to victims’

A similar outcome may follow for 4chan, the controversial anonymous web forum that has also refused to comply with the laws.

Lawyers for the two websites say that they are based in the US and have no British operations; 4chan has sued Ofcom in American courts.

Blocking X would be a different matter altogether.

The company has made efforts to comply with the law, handing Ofcom a risk assessment and introducing age controls that stop children seeing explicit material.

Lorna Woods, a professor of internet law at the University of Essex, says X could face punishment from Ofcom if it had failed to inform the regulator about changes to Grok that have allowed the bot to generate the offending images.

The company could face investigation for allowing the sharing of illegal images.

This week, the Internet Watch Foundation, Britain’s child abuse hotline, said it had seen illegal images, apparently created using Grok and posted on a separate dark web forum.

While creating this is illegal in Britain, it is unclear if X, rather than the user, would be liable, and crucially, the images do not appear to have been published on X itself. Grok allows users to create images privately.

Woods says other deepfake images that were posted on X could constitute illegal content, if they are considered intimate images shared against the subject’s will – a threshold for criminal content under the online safety laws.

However, the mere existence of them would not be enough for punishment. Ofcom would have to show that X had failed to comply with the regulator’s codes of conduct on how to deal with them.

“The codes don’t deal with stopping the problem happening,” says Woods. “The codes are dealing with clearing up afterwards. But that’s all Ofcom can look for the company to do at the moment.”

X, which says it removes child abuse material and suspends accounts that ask it to be created, could argue that it is taking action.

“If they did a 4chan and say ‘we’re not going to play ball, go away’, then I think that increases the likelihood that Ofcom has to do something more dramatic. But X is much more of a business,” adds Woods.

X’s move to limit Grok’s public image posts to premium users was criticised by Downing Street as “insulting to victims”.

Potential ‘fierce’ backlash

While Mr Musk has publicly criticised overseas governments that seek to rein in his company, and has called the Online Safety Act “suppression of the people”, X’s actions have been less combative.

It has complied with Brazilian requests to appoint local representatives to avoid a ban and obeyed Indian takedown orders.

Regulators would also find it impossible to ignore the wider implications of blocking X.

“The backlash to a ban would be fierce, and Starmer would be accused of suppressing free speech,” says Ed Newton-Rex of Fairly Trained, a non-profit that seeks to force AI companies to comply with copyright laws.

He adds that he thinks banning it would be the right move if X doesn’t change course.

Britain would also face potential diplomatic retaliation.

On Christmas Eve, the US State Department barred five Europeans, including Thierry Breton, the architect of the EU’s social media regulations, from entering the US.

Anna Paulina Luna, a Donald Trump ally in the US Congress, said on Friday that Sir Keir should be sanctioned if X is banned in Britain.

However, Technology Secretary Liz Kendall later ramped up the threats. 

“Sexually manipulating images of women and children is despicable and abhorrent. It is an insult and totally unacceptable for Grok to still allow this if you’re willing to pay for it. I expect Ofcom to use the full legal powers Parliament has given them,” she said. 

As long as ministers talk tough, the threat may continue. But given what is at stake, it would be a brave move.

data.title: ​Oliver Marriott, author of The Property Boom who restored the Yorkshire manor house Grimston Garth
data.description:
​Oliver Marriott​, who has died aged 86, was a financial journalist and property company director who restored an exceptional East Yorkshire mansion.

In his early career, Marriott was property editor of Investors Chronicle, financial editor of The Times and the author of well-praised investigative books, The Property Boom (1967) and Anatomy of a Merger (1970).

The former shone light on the machinations of new-rich tycoons in a fast-moving market, ruffling feathers and attracting several legal threats. The latter (co-authored with Robert Jones) explored the amalgamation of the ambitious General Electric Co led by Arnold Weinstock with the more traditional Associated Electrical Industries and English Electric to form a British industrial titan of its era.

In 1969, Marriott left journalism to become a property player in his own right. He joined forces with Jeffrey (later Lord) Sterling and Bruce McPhail in Sterling Guarantee Trust, acquired from the Slater Walker investment group, which they rapidly expanded by acquiring lame-duck companies with attractive assets – Marriott bringing depth of knowledge as the in-house m​arket analyst. 

The 1970 redevelopment of the Gamages department store site in Oxford Street was an early success.

The shrewd Sterling operation survived the crash of 1974 to emerge, with Bank of England and lenders’ support, as the rescuer of the much larger Town & City group – the sector’s biggest potential casualty as owner of Arndale shopping centres and other commercial properties valued at their peak at £600 million. Later, Sterling Guarantee merged with the shipping and property giant P&O, where Marriott was a director until 1991.

Meanwhile, in 1972 Marriott had bought Grimston Garth, a late-18th-century Gothic manor house designed by John Carr of York on a triangular footprint around a central hexagon, with castellated corner towers, in the Holderness district of East Yorkshire. Private, modest and independent in spirit, Marriott delighted in the restoration and care of the Grimston estate for the rest of his life.

Oliver Digby James Marriott was born in West Sussex on August 27 1939, one of four children of Rowland Marriott and his wife Evelyn, née Caillard.

Descended from a line of learned Leicestershire clergymen (though there were also ancestral connections in Yorkshire), Oliver was educated at Eton but shunned the family tradition of going on to study at Oxford. Instead, he became a clerk in an aggregates company in Pimlico and later with Rothschilds in the City.

In 1961 he was victorious as a Labour candidate for Lutterworth Rural District Council in Leicestershire, becoming the youngest councillor in the country. But he was bored with his banking job – and in the same year he discovered his true métier when he joined Investors Chronicle under the wing of its pioneering property editors Charles Gordon and Gary Arnott. 

After a stint on The Statist magazine, he moved in 1965 to The Sunday Times (where the then deputy editor William Rees-Mogg gave him a month off to finish the property book) and was financial editor of The Times from 1967 to 1969.

In his later career, Marriott was founder chairman of two smaller property companies, Churchbury Estates and Ilex, and a non-executive director of Shaftesbury, which owned tracts of Soho and Covent Garden.

A committed Eurosceptic, he stood in the 2005 general election as the Ukip candidate for the Conservative-held Beverley and Holderness constituency, attracting just 4.7 per cent of the poll with 2,336 votes.

His older brother Richard, a City financier who was Lord-Lieutenant of East Yorkshire, restored another historic house, Boynton Hall, a few miles north of Grimston; the brothers shared a particular love of books.

Passionate in his devotion to family life, Oliver Marriott married, in 1964, Lif Kristina Rolandsdottir from Stockholm, who survives him with their two daughters.

​Oliver Marriott, born August 27 1939, died December 5 2025

data.title: How the 50/30/20 budgeting hack can unlock your finances
data.description:
Budgeting is the key to running your finances smoothly. Whether you have a high or low income – or somewhere in between – a budget is important to maintain the levels of spending and saving you can afford.

Getting a handle on your income and outgoings can help reset your finances and spending patterns by assessing where your money goes every month.

A budget can also be a real help in achieving specific financial goals you might have. This could be saving for a summer holiday or a new car, or raising a deposit for a house, a wedding, or even paying off debts.

There are many different budgeting methods to choose from.

The 50/30/20 rule is a straightforward budgeting method that can help you to manage your money simply and effectively so you live within your means and reach your goals – whether that’s to spend less to pay off debts or to save more for your future.

How the 50/30/20 rule works

This method of budgeting suggests dividing your monthly income into three spending categories: 50pc for “needs”, 30pc for “wants” and 20pc for “financial goals”.

The 50pc towards needs will be the portion that covers all the regular essential outgoings such as your mortgage or rent, and household bills such as gas and electricity, water, mobile phone, house insurance, as well as the cost of running a car if you have one.

You would then set aside 30pc for wants, which pays for things like hobbies and sports, socialising, shopping and subscriptions for TV, music streaming or newspapers and magazines.

The remaining 20pc can be used for financial goals, such as paying off debt, contributing to savings, making longer-term investments or saving into a pension.

The 50/30/20 rule can work, in theory, whatever your income level.

Someone earning £34,000 (around the UK national average) would take home £28,000 a year or £2,333 a month, according to calculations by Hargreaves Lansdown, an investment platform.

According to the 50/30/20 rule, this would give you £1,166 a month to spend on needs, £700 on wants, and £467 on savings. With a 5pc pension contribution – which is £142 a month – that would leave you £325 to put aside in the savings pot.

A higher earner on a salary of £60,000 would take home £46,357 a year, or £3,780 a month.

It would give you £1,890 a month to spend on needs, £1,134 on wants and £756 on savings. A 5pc pension contribution – which is £250 a month – means you’re left with £506 to save or repay debts.

If you had a credit card debt of £5,000, you would be able to clear it between 13 and 16 months if you used the budgeting rule.

Equally, this amount could go towards your £20,000 annual tax-free Isa allowance.

Sarah Coles, head of personal finance at Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “How you use the savings money will depend on your circumstances.

“Some will need to pay down expensive short-term debts, while others will want to build emergency savings, their pension or investments – often people will want to do all of these things, so will split the cash between the different goals.”

Bending the budgeting rules

One big problem with this method is that it may not be possible to get all your essential bills into the 50pc bracket.

Ms Coles said: “The proportions won’t work for everyone, largely because the cost of putting a roof over your head is so high in the UK.”

Ms Coles calculated that if you took a middle-income household that earns £32,651 after tax – or £2,721 a month – the cost of their essentials is £1,755 a month, which is 65pc of their income.

If you then put 30pc aside for wants, you’re left with next to nothing to save for the future.

It means you need to flex your “wants” to fit the cash available, and free up enough money for the future, which might work out as 65pc needs, 15pc wants and 20pc savings.

To cut the cost of your “wants”, you’ll need to come up with some sacrifices.

Cutting out one of the restaurant meals you have each month can mean money is redirected to the needs pot, for example. You might also need to ruthlessly cut shopping trips and subscriptions to achieve your goals.

If that doesn’t work for you, you may find that other breakdowns are more achievable – and these could change during the year as life dictates different spending patterns.

Ms Coles added: “Simple rules of thumb like the 50/30/20 can be useful for keeping you roughly on track. However, it’s not a good idea to try to follow them without calculating whether they’ll work for you, or they could lead you astray.”

Timeless budgeting tips

Sticking to a budget can be tricky, and if you’ve already tried a method that didn’t work for you it can be easy to give up.

Budgeting isn’t a one-size-fits-all situation. If you don’t think you can stick to such a strict regime such as the 50/30/20 rule, some of these key budgeting tips can help you stay in control of your money.

1. Understand where your money goes

You might roughly know what you spend your money on each month, but it will help to know your exact outgoings – and what they’re spent on.

If you don’t review your spending often, one way to do this is to go through your bank statements either online or on a banking app and make a list of the bills you pay as well as amounts for online subscriptions, eating out, travel, supermarket shopping, clothes, sports and other treats.

The list might be long, but you can then see where your earnings go – and where any savings can be made.

Many banking apps will automatically categorise your spending so you can easily see whether a large proportion of your money goes on eating out.

If yours doesn’t, then apps such as Moneyhub, Snoop and Emma can do it for you.

2. Know your goals

Each of us has different goals and things we want to save up for – whether it’s a holiday, home improvements or a wedding – or debts to pay off.

Keeping these in mind when thinking about your spending and saving means you have a better chance of reaching them.

3. ‘No spend’ weekends

When an unexpected expense comes your way, such as a car repair or faulty boiler, you may feel the pinch.

A no-spend weekend can be a great way to offset any overspending you’ve done. It involves going 48 hours without tapping any card or buying online. It’s not necessarily about giving up things you enjoy doing, but rather an opportunity to get creative with ways to have fun for free.

This can also be a way of preparing for an expense, boosting your savings.

For an even bigger challenge, you could join the people making “no spend” months popular.

4. Be open about your money mission

Tell friends and family if you’re on a strict money diet so they can support you.

This tactic – also known as “loud budgeting” – can hopefully mean you won’t be embarrassed to opt out of expensive dinners out and any other pricey get-togethers, as you’ll have already made it clear that you’re keeping your spending to a minimum.

5. Seek help if you need it

Some restructuring of your borrowing might be enough to help you regain control.

But if this and some smart budgeting aren’t enough to resolve your money problems, then don’t shy away from seeking help.

Debt advice is best sought from a debt charity, which will advise you, and act on your behalf, with nothing to pay for their services.

Help from a debt charity will have an immediate benefit as they can often negotiate freezing interest payments on some debts to give you some breathing space while you work out a plan for the road ahead.

Beware of debt help or loan consolidation companies that market their services heavily. They will often charge for advice and these fees will only serve to add to your existing debts.

Try the National Debtline (0808 808 4000), StepChange Debt Charity (0800 138 1111), or the Debt Advice Foundation (0800 043 40 50).

data.title: Claire’s collapse thrusts high street undertakers into the spotlight
data.description:
When Claire’s Accessories admitted last week that it faced the prospect of collapse for the second time in just three months, its owner Modella Capital was lightning quick with excuses.

Chief among them: business bogeywoman Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor.

“A combination of very weak consumer confidence, highly adverse government fiscal policies and continued cost inflation” was cited as 1,300 workers at Claire’s returned from Christmas to learn they were facing imminent redundancy.

Administration was “the only option” despite Modella working “intensively” to save the chain, which sells hair clips and jewellery to teenage girls.

It marked a dramatic shift in tone from the cautious optimism that accompanied the self-professed turnaround specialist’s swoop in October.

Back then, Joe Price, the managing director at Modella, spoke enthusiastically about how Claire’s had “the chance to remain on the high street” as he snapped up around 150 of the company’s 300 UK stores.

That sounded optimistic to some observers given the chain’s long history of struggles. But Modella’s appetite and expertise at the toughest end of the high street meant few expected Claire’s to collapse again so soon.

Turnaround firms argue that the nature of the business they’re in inevitably means some survive and some don’t.

Yet with Modella simultaneously planning to pull the plug on another of its investments – discount chain The Original Factory Shop – at the cost of a further 1,200 jobs, the line it straddles between doctor and undertaker risks becoming more blurred.

Clive Black, of Shore Capital, said Claire’s Accessories was a “basket case withering rapidly on the vine”, adding: “Quite what all this means for Modella remains to be seen.”

The restructuring industry prefers to do its fixing away from the spotlight – but following a dizzying deal spree that has seen it sweep up a retail empire at discount prices, Modella has drawn inevitable scrutiny.

In the space of little over a year, beginning in August 2024, Modella hoovered up arts-and-craft chain Hobbycraft, The Original Factory Shop, WH Smith’s high street stores (enigmatically rebranded as TG Jones) and Claire’s Accessories. It has also quietly taken control of Wynsors World of Shoes, which trades mainly in the North of England. All were in varying states of distress.

Modella has seemingly come from nowhere in a few short years to become a major force on the high street, with control of more than 900 shops and an estimated 10,000 staff.

The firm is part of a wider investment outfit called Hay Wain group, set up by former accountant and keen racing driver Jamie Constable. The face of the group is Steve Curtis, a plain-speaking former WH Smith executive and lifelong Chelsea fan.

Curtis chairs Modella and some of the businesses it has bought. He was previously involved in buyouts of card chain Paperchase and womenswear specialist Jigsaw.

Like its rivals, Modella takes an unflinching approach to restructuring, deploying harsh cost-cutting methods that most retailers would shy away from – including the much-maligned company voluntary arrangement (CVA).

Used increasingly by the turnaround industry to force through store closures and rent reductions on landlords to imperilled high street businesses, Modella turned to a CVA to jettison 41 outlets at The Original Factory Shop and impose rent cuts on scores of others.

It also abandoned its Burnley head office and main warehouse, moving to new premises in Bolton and appointing a third party to run its distribution operations. The overhaul is thought to have led to an estimated 1,000 job losses.

A CVA was used to the same effect at Hobbycraft, enabling it to cut 18 shops adrift with the loss of several hundred more jobs.

Senior Modella sources reject suggestions it has chucked in the towel too soon on both The Original Factory Shop and Claire’s.

It had “thrown the kitchen sink” at the former with the chain “in the last chance saloon” when Modella swooped – and it had injected £3m into the business from the outset, one source said.

Its ownership had also got off to a “good start”.

Following the CVA, “we were trading okay – not great guns, a little bit down like-for-like but not too bad”, the same source said.

But a decision to outsource the logistics of The Original Factory Shop backfired and was a major factor in the sudden downturn in trading that cemented its fate.

“The move was pretty dire for us,” a source close to the company said. Problems in its main warehouse with “racking” – how the pallets are stacked – led to “a pretty immediate and significant loss in sales”, the source added.

Sales went from minus 3pc to “double digit negative for a significant period of time” and “the business struggled to recover from that”, the same source said.

At Claire’s, the fatal blow was a failure to persuade landlords to stick with the business, according to Modella sources. Because the chain had been acquired through a pre-pack administration, the stores came with so-called “licences to occupy”, an arrangement that makes it much easier for landlords to take back leases.

According to Modella insiders, 55 landlords said an “outright” no to working with them and a further 42 stores were understood to be preparing to find new tenants – an exodus that would have left behind a business too small to survive, its executives believe.

Turnaround funds have long courted controversy for structuring deals to ensure that they are at the front of the queue when a company folds and the administrators recover any money to hand back to creditors.

While the equity providers and senior lenders often recoup some or all of their investment, smaller creditors and suppliers frequently come away empty-handed from such buyouts.

Modella has taken charge of Claire’s UK assets as security against a loan reportedly worth £7.5m, putting it ahead of other creditors and suppliers to be repaid from the proceeds of any sale of stores and stock.

Meanwhile Retail Realisation, a sister company to Modella within Hay Wain, is in the running to handle the liquidation of both The Original Factory Shop and Claire’s Accessories – a cosy arrangement likely to draw fire from creditors. Fire sales of high street businesses that come with shops and lorries brimming with stock can be lucrative for liquidators.

Rivals are unconvinced by Modella’s account of what went wrong.

“I think they overpaid for Claire’s and The Original Factory Shop so I am not surprised they have had problems,” one said.

“I don’t really think they have a plan [in general] other than ‘buy it cheap and we will make money somehow’,” the person added.

Another questioned whether Modella should have acquired Claire’s in the first place because the business was too small to survive from the outset.

“Not only have you got a business that was bust when they bought it, they took it down to a sub-critical number of stores,” the source said. “So you don’t get the preferential margin rates [from suppliers] that you would have got previously – they bought a business that was never going to work.”

As for the suggestion that the Chancellor was to blame, one retail figure said: “What has Rachel Reeves done in the two and a half months that they’ve owned Claire’s that we weren’t aware of already?”

Questions will also be asked about the future of Modella’s remaining retail investments – particularly the WH Smith stores now operating under the TG Jones fascia.

Under the terms of the deal, Modella is prevented from closing rafts of stores through a CVA during the intervening 12 months. But the agreement expires at the end of March and privately, Modella concedes some of the shops are loss-making.

Hobbycraft is said to be in better shape with the company expected to shortly announce “strong trading” over the six weeks leading up to Christmas.

Despite being thrust into the spotlight in recent days, the appetite of those behind Modella appears not to have been dented.

The Telegraph has learned that the firm is also in talks with retail giant Next about a break-up bid for the 150-year-old family-owned cobbler Russell and Bromley that would see it vanish from the high street.

The plans involve Next buying the brand and Modella affiliate Retail Realisation closing its 37 stores.

On Britain’s dying high streets, undertaking may be the best business to be in.

data.title: Uncovered: Secret room beneath Chinese embassy that poses threat to City
data.description:
China is to build a hidden chamber alongside Britain’s most sensitive communication cables as part of a network of 208 secret rooms beneath its new London “super-embassy”, The Telegraph can reveal.

This newspaper has uncovered detailed plans for an underground complex below the vast diplomatic site in central London, which Beijing has sought to keep from public scrutiny.

Despite the apparent security risk, Sir Keir Starmer is expected to approve the embassy before a visit to China later this month, when he is due to meet Xi Jinping, the Chinese president.

The plans, which are redacted in all publicly available versions, can only be revealed because The Telegraph has uncovered the unredacted documents.

The drawings show that a single concealed chamber will sit directly alongside fibre-optic cables transmitting financial data to the City of London, as well as email and messaging traffic for millions of internet users.

The same hidden room is fitted with hot-air extraction systems, possibly suggesting the installation of heat-generating equipment such as advanced computers used for espionage. The plans also show that China intends to demolish and rebuild the outer basement wall of the chamber, directly beside the fibre-optic cables.

The disclosures will reignite fears that Beijing could be seeking to spy on the information carried through the network, potentially gaining access to state and financial secrets.

Alicia Kearns, the shadow national security minister, claimed that granting approval would hand China “a launchpad for economic warfare at the heart of the central nervous system of our critical national infrastructure”.

She added: “The unredacted plans reveal a concealed room running immediately alongside the fibre-optic cables critical to the City and Canary Wharf. Telegraph readers don’t need me to spell out the obvious threats posed, nor China’s subterfuge – so why does the Labour Government?”

Other areas obscured by redactions include emergency back-up generators, a sprinkler plant, new lift shafts and communications cabling. The plans also conceal bathrooms and showers, potentially allowing officials to remain underground for extended periods.

Covering 22,000 sq m on the site of the former Royal Mint, the embassy would be the largest Chinese diplomatic mission in Europe.

The project has been mired in controversy since Beijing first sought planning permission under the previous Conservative government, not least because of its proximity to sensitive communications infrastructure. Dominic Cummings, Boris Johnson’s former chief adviser, has claimed that MI5 and MI6 warned him China was “trying to build a spy centre underneath the embassy”.

Most of the planning documents submitted to Tower Hamlets council were heavily redacted for what China described as “security reasons”  a move that led Angela Rayner, then housing secretary, to delay approval and demand clarification.

The Telegraph can now reveal the unredacted plans, offering the clearest insight yet into China’s intentions for the hidden areas of its new diplomatic headquarters.

The secret room

Of the 208 rooms exposed by The Telegraph, the most striking lies beneath the Seamen’s Registry building on the north-west corner of the former Royal Mint site.

Its exterior wall borders Mansell Street, a busy artery at the Tower Hill interchange.

Plans show a triangular underground chamber measuring up to 40m across and 2-3m deep. Crucially, they indicate that the outer basement wall facing Mansell Street would be demolished and rebuilt.

It is not yet known which company China will appoint as its contractors to build its planned new embassy, or who will be responsible for demolishing and rebuilding the Seaman’s Registry basement wall.

But that construction work would place Chinese officials just over one metre from the fibre-optic cables running beneath the pavement – raising the prospect that they could be tapped.

Telecoms cable plans seen by The Telegraph show the fibre-optics here belong to companies including BT Openreach, Colt Technologies and US telecoms giant Verizon Business.

Carrying signals bearing the innermost financial secrets of the British economy, the cables stretch between the Telehouse group of data centres in Docklands and other centres around the capital. Linked together, these form the core of the London Internet Exchange (Linx). Beyond London, they connect to Atlantic cables linking to the US.

Linx is one of the biggest internet exchange points in the world, handling vast volumes of data spanning everything from financial transactions to instant messages and emails.

Its cables carry the financial transaction data relied upon by banks to update withdrawals and deposits, such as ordinary people’s salary packets and payments for goods bought online.

Further drawings from the plans of the secret room suggest at least two air-extraction systems will be installed, venting through an existing lightwell and a new grille – implying the need to remove large volumes of hot air from the basement.

One explanation is that China plans to install extensive computer infrastructure as part of an espionage operation linked to the Mansell Street cables.

Professor Alan Woodward, a security expert at the University of Surrey, said: “China won’t say what the basement is for. It could be legitimate classified communications equipment – but that can hide a multitude of sins.”

“If they wanted to tap the cables,” he added, “they wouldn’t need to go far. You wouldn’t know what was happening down there.”

Options for spying on the data could include diverting cables, inserting a wire tap or even placing a device directly on top of the cables. The fibres could even be bent so that light leaks through their casing, allowing it to be read by specialised equipment.

Prof Woodward described the demolition of the basement wall as a “red flag”.

“There’s a long history of cable-tapping by East and West alike. Anyone who can do it has done it.

“Espionage isn’t just about state secrets. Economic intelligence is central to the mission of foreign intelligence services.

“If I were in their shoes, having those cables on my doorstep would be an enormous temptation.”

China may, of course, have benign reasons for installing cooling systems. The space could house a data centre serving embassy staff, or even a gym or cafeteria.

Even so, the proximity to Britain’s most critical communications infrastructure will deepen concern.

The site borders the Wapping Telephone Exchange on three sides. The BT-owned exchange serves an area of roughly 2 sq km, encompassing hundreds of City firms, including Lloyd’s of London, as well as landmarks such as the Gherkin and the Walkie-Talkie.

Nearby cables also connect 11 data centres forming part of the London Internet Exchange.

Any nation state would be tempted to snoop on such traffic – particularly one with which Britain’s relations have been so fraught.

Flats and bathrooms

Plans also show proposals for flats within the Embassy House building on the eastern side of the site.

Detailed layouts suggest luxury accommodation for senior diplomats, alongside smaller studio-style units. There is no obvious evidence of the detention facilities feared by some critics.

Basement drawings reveal further living infrastructure, including toilets and showers at the southern end of Embassy House. Adjacent rooms vary in size, with the largest suitable for a gym. Others, smaller and more secure, lie behind winding corridors and multiple doors.

The plans also show lift shafts, power and communications ducts, and a tunnel linking the Seamen’s Registry to a vast car park beneath the complex.

Utility drawings indicate rooms for high-voltage electricity, water supply and the embassy’s official telecoms connections.

At the northern end is a large space apparently designated for emergency generators, confirmed by a ground-floor drawing marked “New emergency generator ventilation”.

The Government declined to comment on the plans.

Ms Kearns said the embassy posed “obvious threats” to national security.

“To approve this site is to hand China a launchpad for economic warfare at the very heart of our critical financial infrastructure,” she said.

“No one can reassure me that the CCP’s technological capabilities will be containable in a decade – let alone five.”

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said redactions were standard practice in planning appeals.

China’s London embassy did not respond to requests for comment.

BT subsidiary Openreach said it had “robust security measures in place” and worked with the Government to protect its assets.

Explore the unredacted plans in full

data.description:
Join Michael in the comment section below from 11am.

At least 150 teachers have been banned from schools over the last 10 years for child sexual abuse.

The true figure could be considerably higher as Freedom of Information Act requests show that since 2015 a further 256 were struck off for non-defined sexual misconduct and another 245 for “breach of boundaries/trust”.

Yet all these nearly 700 former teachers – indeed all 1,095 teachers who have been disbarred for whatever reason since 2015 – have retained whatever pension entitlements they previously accrued.

The power exists to strip teachers of their pension if they commit treason (thankfully still not the most common of occurrences), serious violations of the Official Secrets Act (there will be little in the way of classified information lying around most schools) or offences committed in connection with their role that could damage confidence in the profession or are “gravely injurious to the state”.

But this power has not been exercised in recent decades. No teacher has lost their pension since at least 2005. The power is also rarely used in relation to others in the public sector – under the NHS pension scheme just 33 people have been stripped of their entitlements since the 1970s.

Now, victims groups are calling for this to change and for those who have so despicably breached the trust placed in them to lose their pensions.

One must have sympathy with this line of argument.

Few sane people would wish to stand up for the rights of paedophiles. Those who abuse children in their care fully deserve to be punished to the full extent the law permits.

There is a very strong case for awarding the pensions of abusers to their victims. Such actions are not just criminal, they are also civil wrongs for which victims are entitled to sue for compensation and seize assets from their abusers.

But in terms of the state stripping people of their pension entitlements, let us be careful what we wish for.

This is a dangerous road to go down, especially in the hands of our current Labour Government and the woke educational and NHS blob. What starts with a clampdown on sex abusers could soon end up with public sector workers losing their pensions for wrong-speak. The legions of the debanked – almost half a million customers last year – could then be joined by the unpensioned.

This is not an argument about the sustainability of public sector pensions and whether the current arrangements can be justified. The Teachers’ Pension Scheme costs taxpayers around £9bn per year. The total liability of public sector pensions is estimated at £5.8tn.

If all these schemes were closed now, annual costs would still rise for decades – peaking at £132bn in 2061.

The whole system is in urgent need of reform, but that is a completely different issue from individuals losing their entitlements for misconduct. In addition, that public sector pensions are such a costly behemoth is not the fault of individual teachers, nurses or doctors. It is a consequence of decades of cowardice by politicians of all parties, finding it easier to avoid having to take difficult decisions.

The reason why unpensioning miscreants is generally wrong, I would argue, is that don’t imagine if ministers start to get a taste for such action they will stop with such offenders. Again and again, policies which have started as a means of combatting a genuine issue have morphed into a much wider bureaucratic power grab.

Just take a look at the Prevent programme at schools, set up to combat Islamist extremism. Examining the scheme now, you might imagine that the biggest domestic threat to British national security did not come from Islamic State adherents and their fellow travellers, but from the extreme Right.

In the year to the end of March 2024, 45pc of referrals were for “extreme Right concerns”, almost double the 23pc referred for “Islamist concerns”. Another 18pc were referred for the rather catch all-sounding “conflicted ideology”.

The case against unpensioning people we wholly disapprove of is the same as why we should champion free speech for those with views we abhor.

Standing up for the rights of people we broadly agree with to express their views is simple. The real test is whether we are willing to stand up for the rights of those whose views we hate.

It is the easiest thing in the world for someone of my world view to be outraged if a university bans a speaker for their Brexit-y, gender critical or pro-Israel views. It is much more difficult – and for that reason all the more important – to stand up for freedom of expression for trans activists and those taking, say, a virulent anti-Israel stance.

If we clamour for the unpensioning of those who are genuinely reprehensible, we will have handed another tool to the Whitehall machine to regulate behaviour they don’t approve of. And who knows who will be next in their sights.

By past performance, it could well be those expressing views which run counter to the prevailing consensus. And those views will generally be of a conservative persuasion.

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.

data.title: US billionaires fleeing Trump snap up cut-price London mansions
data.description:
Billionaires fleeing Donald Trump’s America are snapping up cut-price London mansions from non-doms.

American buyers were behind one in five luxury home sales in London last year and are expected to continue to be a significant driver of purchases in 2026, according to Beauchamp Estates’ annual Billionaire Buyers in London report.

Gary Hersham, the founder of Beauchamp Estates, told The Telegraph that American buyers had become the “most serious” group in the £15m-plus market, with many “disillusioned” by Mr Trump’s presidency.

“I think it impacts the way Americans are thinking,” he said. “A lot of them want to leave the US and come to England.”

High-profile Trump exiles include TV host Ellen DeGeneres, who has said she would live in the Cotswolds after his re-election as president.

George Lucas, the Star Wars creator, last year spent £40m on a mansion in St John’s Wood, prompting speculation that he too may be relocating for political reasons.

Last year, 41 homes priced above £15m sold in London. Around 65pc of sales were non-doms quitting Britain for lower-tax destinations such as Dubai and Monaco, according to Beauchamp.

‘It does not match what you can get in London’

That exodus has put downward pressure on prices, and Mr Hersham said US buyers were also attracted by the value on offer in London.

He said: “If you look at what they’ve bought in London, the quality is exceptional compared with America; it’s a pittance compared to their wealth. Americans actually really like London and realise it’s a very good idea to diversify their assets.”

The gap between the average asking price and sale price widened to 7.6pc last year, compared to 7.1pc in 2024 and 5.6pc in 2023.

Mr Hersham added: “If you look at what you can buy for your money in Los Angeles or in New York in terms of style and quality, it does not match what you can get in London.”

Beauchamp Estates predicted that mansion prices will weaken by between 2pc and 3pc this year. Mr Hersham said some wealthy buyers continued to “view London with some caution” following recent tax and regulatory changes.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, unveiled a mansion tax on properties worth more than £2m in her November Budget. Ms Reeves has also set out plans to end non-dom status.

While Trump exiles are a significant part of the luxury London property market, Middle Eastern buyers are likely to continue to account for most activity this year, according to Beauchamp.

Gulf investors who have recently bought mansions in London include Abu Dhabi billionaire Hussain Sajwani, who bought a townhouse overlooking Hyde Park for £28m in spring last year.

data.title: Drug-driving causes more road deaths than alcohol
data.description:
Drugs are now a bigger factor in road deaths than alcohol, official figures show.

The number of deceased drivers who tested positive for drugs increased by 78 per cent – from 106 to 189 – in the decade to 2023, according to the Department for Transport (DfT) and police data.

By contrast, the number of dead motorists with alcohol proven to be present in their system rose by five per cent in the same period, from 162 to 171.

Sir Keir Starmer now stands accused of ignoring the bigger problem of drug-driving while planning to reduce the drink-driving limit. The Government’s proposals have prompted a backlash from MPs and publicans, who say they will put pubs under more pressure following an increase in business rates.

Britain lost an average of one pub each day in 2025, and industry bosses have warned that rising tax bills and wages, on top of higher energy costs, will drive hundreds more out of business.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save the UK’s pubs, calling on Labour to stop its assault on locals and cut tax and red tape.

Ministers are now expected to reverse their decision to impose higher costs on Britain’s struggling pubs, saying they are working on relief measures to be announced in the coming days. But the about-turn relates to jumps in business rates for landlords, not the new drink-drive limit.

DfT figures show that the percentage of fatal collisions in which drink-driving was involved has been relatively stable over the past 10 years, at 13 per cent.

However, the proportion in which drug-driving played a role doubled from five per cent in 2014 to 10 per cent in 2023.

While drug-driving convictions rose by 13.5 per cent to 27,000 in 2024, the number of drivers convicted of drink-driving offences fell by six per cent to 36,415.

Meanwhile, injuries from drink-driving incidents have significantly decreased since 1980, from around 20,000 annually to about 5,000 since 2020.

Despite this, the Government’s new road strategy proposes “taking tougher action on drink-driving” by reducing the legal limit of 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg, or around a pint.

It would be the most significant reform to road safety laws since 1967, when the blood alcohol limit was first introduced.

Chris Philp, the shadow home secretary, said: “Labour are now proposing even more measures that will endanger country pubs.

“At the same time, the Government is completely failing to do more to address a more rapidly growing road safety issue – drug-driving. More drivers killed in a collision had drugs in their system than alcohol.

“The Government should prioritise toughening up on drug-drivers above measures which will strangle struggling country pubs.”

The British Beer and Pub Association warned that any toughening of measures on drink-driving would harm rural pubs in areas without public transport or reliable taxi services.

A spokesman said: “The pub sector continues to face huge challenges, so any additional policy measures that further impact trade will be of real concern to licensees, especially those in rural areas.”

‘Geographical lottery’

Drug-drivers face similar penalties as those caught drinking, including a minimum 12-month driving ban and up to six months in prison for serious or repeat offences.

Limits for illegal drugs such as cannabis, cocaine, ketamine and heroin are set at extremely low levels, but not at zero in order to account for accidental exposure.

However, Government-funded research has suggested that dangerous drug-drivers have been escaping prosecution – and putting lives at risk – because some police forces ration the number of testing kits issued to officers to just one a day.

The study, by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, found there was a “geographical lottery” where the best-performing forces were catching 10 times more drug-drivers per head of population than the worst.

Drivers can also escape justice because of delays of four to five months in processing blood tests. Officers have only six months to prosecute.

Penalties to be reviewed

The Government’s new road safety strategy proposes that there should be a review of penalties and mandatory training for drink and drug-driving offences.

It has also pledged to explore alternative processing and evidence collection for drug-driving to “improve speed of results, supporting more robust enforcement outcomes.”

A DfT spokesman said the strategy would “save thousands of lives by targeting the root causes of deaths, including the impact of both alcohol and drugs”.

They added: “We’re determined to crack down on drug-driving, and the strategy includes new measures to modernise how we tackle it, including new testing methods, and powers to suspend driving licenses for those caught under the influence.

“We do not expect the new limit to harm pubs – experience in Scotland shows such changes have minimal impact on local businesses while making roads safer.”

Government sources also denied that the strategy failed to focus on tackling drug-driving, pointing to plans for roadside tests to catch drug-drivers to be stepped up.

Motorists suspected of driving under the influence of drugs would also have their licences suspended immediately under the plans. Roadside tests to catch drug-drivers will also be stepped up.

Police can already use roadside tests as a preliminary check, but results must be confirmed by requiring the driver to take a blood test in a lab. The strategy will set out plans to allow the police to charge suspects based solely on a roadside test.

However, ministers accept that this power will only be used when more reliable tests, that can assess exact amounts of drugs in people’s blood, are available. They also want to encourage police to use roadside tests more widely.

data.title: Billionaire Labour donor pays himself £66m
data.description:
The Liverpudlian tycoon and Labour donor behind Home Bargains has paid himself and his family a £66m dividend as his budget retail chain embarks on a major expansion.

Tom Morris, who is nicknamed “the richest Scouser to have ever lived”, received the payout from his company, TJ Morris Group, last year, according to newly published accounts.

The 71-year-old’s payout capped a strong year that saw sales at his Home Bargains empire grow from £4.2bn to £4.5bn, with profits rising from £454m to £523m.

It also follows a much larger £1.2bn dividend payment from subsidiary company TJ Morris Ltd that he took home in 2024. Mr Morris personally owns 90pc of the shares of his holding company, with various family trusts holding the rest.

Home Bargains promises “top brands, bargain prices” and sells everything from discounted toys to bedspreads and bird feeders, competing with the likes of B&M and Poundstretcher across the country.

The chain had 632 outlets at the end of last June, up from 617 a year earlier, and is targeting a total of 800 to 1,000 eventually, with its latest expansion focused on the South.

Home Bargains’ success has propelled Mr Morris to become one of Britain’s richest businessmen – and biggest taxpayers – with his family commanding a combined fortune of almost £7bn, according to the Sunday Times Rich List.

He owns a $50m (£38m) Bombardier Global 5000 private jet with M-ORIS spelt out on the tail, a $45m superyacht called Moskito and a $10m VIP Bell 429 helicopter.

Mr Morris is one of seven children born to a Liverpool shopkeeper and has grown Home Bargains from a single store in east Liverpool that he opened in 1976. His brother, Joseph, is operations director.

The billionaire has also donated to the Labour Party in recent years, awarding thousands of pounds to the campaign funds of senior figures, including Wes Streeting.

He gave £5,000 to Mr Streeting, the Health Secretary, in 2023 ahead of the following year’s general election.

Mr Streeting has since emerged as a potential contender for prime minister and Labour leader, amid concerns in the party about Sir Keir Starmer’s dire poll ratings.

In 2017, Mr Morris also donated £10,000 to Steve Rotherham to support his ultimately successful campaign to be Liverpool’s metro mayor.

The billionaire was previously scrutinised for failing to repay taxpayer support that Home Bargains received during the pandemic.
